proposals.title: Breaking the Sedentary Pattern: A Randomized Crossover Trial Comparing the Acute Glycemic and Insulinemic Impacts of Ambulatory vs. Non-Ambulatory Activity Breaks in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence strongly supports breaking prolonged sitting with ambulatory activity (e.g., walking) to improve postprandial glucose and insulin. However, this may not be feasible in many occupational or domestic settings. It is unknown whether non-ambulatory, desk-based activities (e.g., resistance exercises, seated marching) can provide comparable metabolic benefits, a critical gap for developing practical public health guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that brief, frequent bouts of non-ambulatory muscle activity will be as effective as ambulatory walking breaks in attenuating postprandial glucose and insulin excursions during prolonged sitting in individuals at risk for type 2 diabetes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, randomized crossover trial with 25 adults exhibiting sedentary behavior and elevated type 2 diabetes risk. Participants will complete three 7-hour conditions: prolonged sitting; sitting interrupted with 3-minute light-intensity walking breaks every 30 minutes; and sitting interrupted with 3-minute bouts of seated resistance exercises (calf raises, knee extensions) every 30 minutes. Continuous glucose monitoring, serial venous blood draws for insulin, and wearable electromyography (EMG) on major leg muscles will be used. Primary outcomes are incremental area under the curve for glucose and insulin. EMG data will be processed to quantify muscle activity volume (amplitude * duration).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, mechanistic comparison of ambulatory and non-ambulatory break strategies on acute glycemia. It will clarify whether the key mechanism is ambulation per se or simply the contractile activity of large muscle groups. The findings will directly inform the design of feasible 'sit-less' interventions for office and home environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes the benefit of walking breaks, this study innovatively tests the sufficiency of non-ambulatory muscle contractions, integrating precise EMG-based quantification of muscle activity with metabolic outcomes to move beyond a simple behavioral prescription to a physiological principle.

proposals.title: The Chrono-Efficacy of Activity Breaks: A Randomized Crossover Trial Comparing Postprandial Glucose and Insulin Responses to Sedentary Interruptions After Morning, Lunch, and Evening Meals
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including work by Owen and others, has established that breaking prolonged sitting with light activity improves postprandial glucose and insulin. However, studies have predominantly tested these breaks in the morning or across a standardized day, neglecting potential circadian variations in metabolism and muscle insulin sensitivity. It remains unknown if the metabolic benefit of activity breaks is consistent throughout the waking day, which is critical for personalized, time-sensitive public health guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficacy of activity breaks in attenuating postprandial glucose and insulin excursions will be greatest following the morning meal compared to breaks taken after lunch or dinner, due to underlying diurnal metabolic rhythms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, randomized crossover trial in adults with overweight/obesity and elevated metabolic risk. Participants will complete three 7-hour conditions: (1) Morning-break (post-breakfast), (2) Afternoon-break (post-lunch), and (3) Evening-break (post-dinner). Each condition will involve frequent, prolonged sitting interrupted by 3-minute bouts of light-intensity walking every 30 minutes, timed to commence immediately after the standardized meal for that condition. Continuous glucose monitors and serial venous blood draws will measure glucose and insulin. Actigraphy and wearable electromyography (as per Owen's recent methods) will objectively quantify sedentary time and muscle activity. Statistical analysis will use linear mixed models to compare incremental areas under the curve (iAUC) for glucose and insulin across conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence on whether the timing of activity breaks modulates their acute cardiometabolic benefits. It will clarify if 'morning is the only relevant time for testing' or if breaks are equally valuable later in the day. The findings will directly inform more precise physical activity guidelines for sedentary interruption, moving beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' approach to consider chronobiology.
proposals.novelty: While the general benefit of activity breaks is known, applying a rigorous chronobiological lens to test their differential efficacy across meal times is novel. It integrates Owen's expertise in sedentary behavior, glucose metabolism, and emerging wearable sensor methods (fNIRS, EMG) to address a fundamental, unanswered question in behavioral physiology.

proposals.title: The acute and cumulative effects of regular activity breaks on vascular function and blood pressure in adults with elevated cardiometabolic risk: a randomized crossover trial
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease, with established links to impaired glucose and insulin metabolism. However, the impact of breaking up sitting time on other key cardiometabolic pathways, particularly vascular function and blood pressure, is less clear. Understanding these effects is crucial for developing comprehensive public health guidelines to mitigate the cardiovascular risks of sedentary behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that frequent, brief bouts of light-intensity walking during prolonged sitting will lead to acute improvements in endothelial function (measured by flow-mediated dilation) and reductions in ambulatory blood pressure, and that these benefits will be sustained with repeated daily exposure over one week.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, three-condition crossover laboratory trial in 25 adults with overweight/obesity and elevated fasting glucose. Conditions will be: 1) uninterrupted sitting (8hr), 2) sitting interrupted with 3-min light walking every 30-min, and 3) sitting interrupted with 6-min light walking every 60-min. Brachial artery flow-mediated dilation (FMD) and continuous blood pressure will be measured pre-, during, and post-intervention. A subset will then complete a 5-day free-living phase replicating their assigned break pattern, monitored via thigh-worn accelerometry (activPAL) and continuous glucose monitors, with 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure measured on day 1 and 5. Data will be analyzed using linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel evidence on the dose-response relationship between activity break frequency/duration and direct measures of vascular health. It will clarify whether the glucoregulatory benefits of breaking up sitting translate to improvements in the vascular system, a critical pathway for cardiovascular disease prevention. The findings will inform more precise physical activity guidelines for sedentary populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Owen and others has focused on glucose and insulin, this study uniquely integrates high-fidelity laboratory measures of vascular function with a multi-day free-living protocol to assess both acute mechanisms and real-world sustainability of effects on blood pressure and endothelial health.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Temporal Dynamics of Postprandial Glycemia in Response to Real-World Sedentary and Activity Breaks: A Free-Living Ambulatory Study Using Continuous Glucose Monitoring and Wearable Sensors
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting and poor sleep are established risk factors for impaired glucose metabolism and type 2 diabetes, but their combined, real-time effects in free-living settings are poorly quantified. Pre-2026 research by Owen and others has linked sedentary time and sleep to glycemia, yet most evidence relies on laboratory protocols or intermittent blood sampling, missing the dynamic, within-day interplay of behaviors and glucose. Continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) now enable detailed, ambulatory assessment, creating an opportunity to move beyond associations to characterize temporal dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in adults with overweight or prediabetes, the magnitude and duration of postprandial glucose excursions will be significantly greater following prolonged, uninterrupted sitting periods compared to days interspersed with frequent, short bouts of light-intensity activity, and that these effects will be moderated by prior-night sleep quality.
proposals.method: We will recruit 50 adults at risk for type 2 diabetes for a 14-day free-living observational study. Participants will wear a CGM (e.g., Dexcom G6), a thigh-worn accelerometer (activPAL) to classify sitting, standing, and stepping, and a wrist-worn actigraph (ActiGraph GT9X) to measure sleep. Using time-synchronized data, we will apply functional data analysis and multilevel regression models to quantify the glucose area under the curve (AUC) following participant-identified meals. Key predictors will be the preceding 60-minute sedentary bout duration and the frequency of activity breaks, with sleep efficiency as a moderating variable in cross-classified models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel, high-resolution evidence on how real-world sedentary and activity patterns temporally shape glycemic control outside the lab. It will extend Owen's work on 'muscle silence' by directly linking it to glucose dynamics and integrate sleep as a moderating factor, offering a holistic behavioral model. Findings will inform precise, context-aware interventions to break sitting for glycemic benefit.
proposals.novelty: While CGMs and activity monitors have been used separately, the novelty lies in applying functional temporal analysis to their synchronized, free-living data streams to model the *dynamic, within-day* effect of sedentary behavior patterns on glucose, explicitly testing sleep as a moderator in an ecological setting.

proposals.title: Objective Quantification of Activity Break Adherence and Intensity Using Wearable Accelerometers: Associations with Postprandial Glucose and Insulin Dynamics in Adults with Sedentary Lifestyles
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is an established risk factor for impaired glucose metabolism and type 2 diabetes. While breaking up sitting time with light activity is beneficial, real-world trials rely on self-reported adherence, which is unreliable. There is a critical need for objective, granular measurement of break frequency, duration, and intensity to understand the precise dose-response relationship with metabolic health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that objectively measured higher frequency and greater intensity of activity breaks will be independently associated with lower postprandial glucose and insulin responses, and that these associations will be stronger than those based on self-reported break data.
proposals.method: We will deploy wrist-worn tri-axial accelerometers (ActiGraph GT9X) on 120 sedentary adults for 7 days, including a standardized meal test day. Raw acceleration data will be processed using validated algorithms (e.g., GGIR) to derive sedentary bouts and breaks. Break adherence will be quantified as the number of interruptions to sedentary bouts ≥30 minutes. Break intensity will be classified using machine-learned random forest models, trained on lab-based activities, to categorize breaks as light (e.g., standing, slow walking) or moderate-vigorous. These metrics will be analyzed against continuous glucose monitor (CGM) and serum insulin measures from the meal test.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first objective, algorithm-derived profile of real-world activity break 'dosing'. It will establish precise, device-measured thresholds for break frequency and intensity that are linked to acute glycemic benefits, moving the field beyond simple time-in-motion estimates. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective and precisely measurable sedentary behavior interventions.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond simply measuring 'sedentary time' or 'light activity' to specifically algorithmically detect and characterize the *breaks* themselves as discrete behavioral events, linking their precise properties to metabolic outcomes, which is a novel application of wearable data processing.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Pathways: A Multi-Exposure Isolation Framework to Partition the Glycemic Impact of Television Viewing into Inactivity, Snacking, and Stress Components
proposals.motivation: Television viewing is a prevalent, high-volume sedentary behavior robustly associated with cardiometabolic risk, including poor glycemic control. However, it remains a 'black box' exposure, conflating prolonged sitting, frequent energy-dense snacking, and cognitive-emotional stress. Pre-2026 research has not successfully quantified the independent mechanistic contributions of these co-occurring exposures, limiting targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the acute postprandial glycemic impact of a TV viewing session is significantly mediated by concurrent energy intake and psychological stress, independent of the postural inactivity component. The stress pathway will be reflected in autonomic dysregulation (reduced heart rate variability).
proposals.method: In a controlled, within-subjects study, adults with overweight and elevated type 2 diabetes risk will undergo three 2-hour conditions in random order: TV viewing, passive sitting (matched inactivity without TV), and unstructured time. We will use concurrent, high-frequency monitoring: thigh-worn accelerometers (activPAL) for posture; continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and pre/post blood draws for insulin; wrist-worn ECG (e.g., E4) for heart rate variability (HRV); and ecological momentary assessment for stress and dietary recall. A causal mediation analysis framework will be applied, treating TV viewing as the exposure, with inactivity duration, caloric intake (from EMA), and HRV metrics as parallel mediators, and glycemic area under the curve (AUC) as the primary outcome.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimates of the distinct pathways linking TV viewing to acute dysglycemia, moving beyond association to mechanistic attribution. It will generate novel evidence to inform whether public health guidelines should target snacking behaviors or stress reduction during screen time, in addition to promoting breaks in sitting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the multi-exposure isolation framework from environmental epidemiology to behavioral science and using a multi-sensor, within-subject design to statistically partition the effect of a complex behavioral exposure into its constituent, measurable pathways.

proposals.title: Estimating the population-level life expectancy impact of total recreational screen time: A modeling study using device-measured sedentary behavior and cardiometabolic biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence strongly links device-measured sedentary time with adverse cardiometabolic outcomes, including impaired glucose and insulin dynamics. However, the specific, population-level mortality burden of pervasive recreational screen time (computers, smartphones, tablets) remains poorly quantified, limiting targeted public health guidance. This gap is critical as screen-based sedentarism becomes the dominant leisure-time behavior in many societies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that total daily recreational screen time is a significant, independent predictor of reduced life expectancy at a population level, mediated through its association with prolonged, uninterrupted sedentary bouts and their established detrimental effects on glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will extend our established model linking sedentary behavior to diabetes risk (from 'Silence of the Muscles') to estimate life expectancy impact. Using pooled data from existing cohort studies with device-measured sedentarism (activPAL) and self-reported screen time, we will calibrate a dose-response relationship. This will inform a life-table analysis model, estimating Years of Life Lost (YLL) attributable to recreational screen time. The model will integrate known associations between sedentary time, glycemic biomarkers (fasting glucose, insulin, HbA1c), and all-cause mortality risk, using mediation analysis techniques to partition direct and indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative estimate of the mortality burden of total recreational screen time, moving beyond associations with intermediate biomarkers. It will deliver a publicly accessible model for policymakers to project the health impact of population-level changes in screen use. The findings will directly inform the development of specific, evidence-based screen time guidelines for adults.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links *general* sedentarism to health, this proposal uniquely quantifies the life expectancy impact of the specific, modifiable domain of *recreational screen time*. It innovatively integrates device-measured sedentary patterns, self-reported behavioral context, and cardiometabolic pathway data into a single, policy-relevant modeling framework.

proposals.title: The Metabolic Dose-Response of Sedentary Interruptions: Investigating the Efficacy of Very Brief, Low-Intensity Breaks on Postprandial Glucose and Insulin
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence shows that interrupting prolonged sitting improves postprandial glucose and insulin. However, public health and workplace guidelines need to know the minimal effective 'dose'. It is unclear if very brief (e.g., 1-minute) or low-intensity (e.g., standing, slow stepping) interruptions provide meaningful metabolic benefits or if returns diminish below a threshold of duration or intensity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the metabolic benefit of interrupting sitting exhibits a dose-response relationship, such that very brief (<2 min) and low-intensity (standing, minimal movement) interruptions will yield significantly smaller improvements in postprandial glucose and insulin compared to longer (≥5 min) or light-intensity (slow walking) interruptions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, crossover laboratory trial in adults with overweight/obesity and elevated type 2 diabetes risk. Participants will complete four 7-hour conditions: prolonged sitting; sitting interrupted with 2-min standing breaks every 30 min; sitting interrupted with 2-min slow-walking breaks; and sitting interrupted with 5-min slow-walking breaks. Continuous glucose monitors, serial insulin measures, and thigh-worn accelerometers/EMG will capture metabolic and muscular activity. The primary analysis will use linear mixed models to compare incremental AUC for glucose and insulin across conditions, with EMG-derived muscle activity as a potential mediator.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental evidence defining a lower threshold for the metabolic efficacy of sedentary breaks. It will inform more precise public health and clinical recommendations for interrupting sitting, particularly for populations with limited mobility or demanding sedentary work environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares sitting to active breaks, this study systematically isolates and tests the independent and interactive effects of break *duration* and *intensity* at the very low end of the spectrum, using objective EMG to link muscle contractile activity directly to metabolic outcomes.

proposals.title: Validating Wearable Electromyography for the Detection of Optimal Physiological Windows for Sitting Interruptions in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes Risk
proposals.motivation: Prolonged, uninterrupted sitting is a significant risk factor for poor glycemic control and Type 2 Diabetes (T2D). While activity breaks are beneficial, their timing relative to underlying muscle silence and metabolic state is poorly understood. Wearable electromyography (EMG) offers a direct, objective measure of muscle inactivity, but its ability to identify specific, real-world moments where an interruption would be most physiologically opportune remains unvalidated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, prolonged patterns of low-level thigh muscle EMG activity (e.g., sustained silence >30 minutes) will be reliably detectable by a wearable EMG system and will correspond to periods of elevated postprandial glucose and insulin resistance, thereby defining optimal windows for beneficial activity breaks.
proposals.method: In a controlled laboratory study, 40 adults at risk for T2D will wear thigh-mounted EMG sensors (e.g., Delsys Trigno) and a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) during an 8-hour simulated office day. We will algorithmically define 'opportunity windows' from EMG as periods of sustained muscle quiescence (>30 min) using root-mean-square and zero-crossing rate thresholds. The primary validation will correlate the onset and duration of these EMG-defined windows with concurrent CGM-derived glucose excursions and frequently sampled insulin sensitivity indices (e.g., Matsuda index) from oral glucose tolerance tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first validation of a wearable EMG-derived biomarker for timing sitting interruptions, moving beyond simple duration metrics. It will contribute a novel, physiologically-grounded framework for 'just-in-time' adaptive interventions aimed at improving glycemic control by targeting breaks when they are likely to have the greatest metabolic impact.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links total sedentary time to glycemia, this study uniquely uses high-fidelity wearable EMG to identify *within-day temporal patterns* of muscle inactivity that signal declining metabolic state, offering a dynamic, personalized target for intervention not possible with actigraphy alone.

proposals.title: Inferring Sedentary Bout Duration and Metabolic Impact from Wearable Electromyography 'Silence' Signatures: An Inverse Modelling Approach
proposals.motivation: Prolonged muscle inactivity ('silence') is a core mechanism linking sedentary time to adverse metabolic outcomes like insulin resistance. Current methods to quantify sedentary behavior (e.g., accelerometry) infer posture and lack direct, continuous measurement of muscle activity. Wearable electromyography (EMG) provides this direct signal but is primarily used to detect activity; its potential to model the duration and metabolic cost of sitting bouts from baseline silence patterns remains unexplored, limiting our ability to characterize the most metabolically detrimental sedentary patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: That the temporal structure and amplitude of baseline EMG 'silence' from key postural muscles (e.g., vastus lateralis) can be used to accurately predict the duration of uninterrupted sitting bouts and estimate their associated acute metabolic cost, as indicated by interstitial glucose fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will collect high-frequency, multi-site wearable EMG (e.g., Delsys Trigno) and continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data from adults with type 2 diabetes or prediabetes during controlled lab sessions and free-living periods. The inverse model will treat EMG silence (amplitude < 2% MVC) as the input. We will develop a time-series regression model, potentially using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network architecture, to map sequences of silence features (duration, variance, co-activation patterns) to bout duration (ground-truthed by thigh-worn accelerometry and video) and to subsequent CGM-derived glycemic excursions (e.g., glucose area under the curve).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physiologically grounded method for characterizing sedentary behavior directly from its proposed mechanistic pathway—muscle inactivity. It will yield a predictive model that translates a biological signal into estimates of behavioral exposure and acute metabolic cost, moving beyond simple detection to quantitative prediction. This could enable more precise, mechanism-targeted interventions and personal risk profiling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Owen and others has established the link between EMG silence and metabolic risk, this study uniquely inverts the problem: instead of using EMG to validate sedentary time, it uses the silence signal itself as the primary data source to predict bout duration and metabolic impact, a fundamentally different analytical paradigm.

proposals.title: Postural Muscle Activation versus Activity Breaks: A Randomized Crossover Trial Comparing Effects on Postprandial Glucose and Insulin Dynamics in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes Risk
proposals.motivation: Sedentary behavior research has focused on interrupting sitting with brief activity bouts. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests that very-low-level muscle activation, even while seated, can increase energy expenditure and improve vascular function. It is unknown whether sustained postural muscle engagement, achievable through ergonomic or behavioral cues, provides superior metabolic benefits compared to the standard interruption paradigm for glycemic control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained, very-low-level electromyographic (EMG) activity in postural muscles during prolonged sitting, induced by an optimized upright posture, will lead to a more favorable postprandial glucose and insulin response than an equivalent period of sitting interrupted by brief bouts of light-intensity walking.
proposals.method: We will conduct a lab-based, randomized three-condition crossover trial in 25 adults with overweight/obesity. Conditions: 1) uninterrupted sitting (control), 2) sitting interrupted with 3-min light walking every 30 minutes, and 3) sustained optimal posture sitting (upright, lumbar support, scapular retraction). Continuous wearable EMG (on lumbar erector spinae and vastus lateralis) will quantify muscle activity. A standardized mixed-meal tolerance test will be administered. Primary outcomes are 3-hour incremental area under the curve for glucose and insulin, measured via venous cannula. EMG data will be processed using root mean square algorithms in 1-minute epochs to compare total integrated muscle activity between conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental comparison of two distinct anti-sedentary strategies—interruptions versus sustained posture—on acute cardiometabolic endpoints. It will generate novel data on the dose (intensity*duration) of muscle activity required for glycemic benefit, informing more precise public health and clinical guidelines for sedentary time management.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant 'break up sitting' paradigm by testing if the quality and continuity of sitting posture itself, quantified via objective EMG, can be a potent metabolic intervention, moving beyond the simple frequency of interruptions.

proposals.title: Effects of Passive Neuromuscular Electrical Stimulation on Postprandial Glucose and Insulin in Sedentary Adults: A Proof-of-Concept Study
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting time is a robust, independent risk factor for impaired glucose metabolism and type 2 diabetes. While voluntary movement interruptions (e.g., brief walking breaks) are effective, adherence is a major barrier. Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work on 'silent muscles,' highlights the need for strategies that do not rely on conscious volition to mitigate the metabolic harms of sedentariness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that passive, low-intensity neuromuscular electrical stimulation (NMES) applied to the quadriceps and hamstrings during a prolonged sitting bout will significantly attenuate postprandial glucose and insulin excursions compared to a control sitting condition without stimulation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, controlled, crossover laboratory study in 20 sedentary adults. Participants will complete two, 7-hour conditions: sitting with NMES (intermittent, sub-tetanic stimulation) and sitting without (control). Standardized meals will be provided. Continuous glucose monitoring, serial insulin measures, and wearable electromyography (to quantify muscle activity) will be primary outcomes. Prefrontal hemodynamics will be monitored via functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) to assess cognitive load and mood, ensuring the intervention is truly 'passive'.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence on whether passive NMES can serve as an effective, adherence-friendly countermeasure to sitting-induced metabolic dysregulation. It will extend the 'muscle activity' paradigm beyond voluntary movement and offer preliminary mechanistic data on muscle glucose disposal and central nervous system response during passive stimulation.
proposals.novelty: While NMES is used in rehabilitation, its application as a passive, low-intensity metabolic disruptor of sedentary behavior is novel. This approach directly tests the principle of decoupling muscle metabolic activity from voluntary movement intention, a non-obvious extension of the 'sit less, move more' paradigm.

proposals.title: Efficacy of a Wearable-Triggered, Micro-Activity Interruption Protocol for Improving 24-Hour Glycemic Control in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes: A Free-Living Randomized Crossover Trial
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a robust risk factor for poor cardiometabolic health, including elevated postprandial glucose and insulin resistance. While laboratory studies show benefits of interrupting sitting with light activity, evidence from long-term, free-living settings is limited, and the optimal 'dose' (frequency, duration, intensity) for real-world efficacy remains unclear. Translating lab-based protocols into effective, sustainable interventions is a critical next step for public health.
proposals.hypothesis: A free-living intervention using a wearable device to prompt frequent, brief bouts of simple resistance activities (e.g., bodyweight squats, calf raises) will lead to greater improvements in 24-hour glycemic control (measured by continuous glucose monitoring) and insulin sensitivity compared to a standard 'sit less, move more' advice control condition in adults with type 2 diabetes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 4-week randomized crossover trial in 50 adults with type 2 diabetes. The intervention uses a wrist-worn device (e.g., GENEActiv) with a custom algorithm to detect sedentary bouts ≥30 minutes and deliver a vibration prompt. The prompt initiates a 3-minute micro-activity protocol of bodyweight exercises. Continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and thigh-worn accelerometers (to validate posture) will collect primary outcome data. Insulin sensitivity will be assessed via fasting blood draws and HOMA-IR. The control condition involves standard lifestyle advice. Mixed-effects models will compare 24-hour mean glucose, glycemic variability, and insulin measures between conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first free-living evidence on the efficacy of a device-prompted, specific activity protocol for glycemic management in a clinical population. It will generate practical knowledge on the feasibility and metabolic impact of interrupting sitting with structured, time-efficient activities outside the lab, directly informing the design of scalable behavioral interventions.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond generic 'break up sitting' advice to test a precise, wearable-enforced protocol of simple resistance activities in a free-living, at-risk population, integrating continuous glucose monitoring and validated posture detection to capture real-world, 24-hour cardiometabolic effects.

proposals.title: Biomechanical Joint Loading During Sedentary Transitions: A Novel Pathway Linking Postural Dynamics to Glycemic Control in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a recognized risk factor for poor glycemic control and type 2 diabetes (T2D), but the underlying mechanisms are not fully understood. While metabolic and muscular inactivity are key, the biomechanical loading of joints and muscles during the transitions between sitting, standing, and stepping—frequent events in daily life—may provide a novel physiological signal influencing glucose metabolism, a pathway underexplored in pre-2026 sedentary behavior research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that greater and more frequent loading of major lower-limb joints (ankles, knees, hips) during transitions from sitting to standing and to stepping, quantified via biomechanical modeling, will be associated with improved postprandial glucose and insulin responses in adults with T2D, independent of total activity volume.
proposals.method: We will recruit 50 adults with T2D. Participants will undergo a standardized laboratory protocol involving motion capture (Vicon system) of sit-stand and sit-walk transitions, synchronized with wearable electromyography (EMG) of quadriceps and gluteal muscles. Using inverse dynamics modeling in OpenSim, we will calculate peak joint reaction forces, joint moments, and muscle forces for each transition. These biomechanical measures will be correlated with continuous glucose monitor (CGM) metrics and insulin sensitivity (from oral glucose tolerance tests) collected over a subsequent 48-hour free-living period monitored by thigh-worn accelerometers (activPAL) to assess posture and transitions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence linking the biomechanical quality of sedentary breaks to glycemic physiology, moving beyond simple duration metrics. It will generate a novel biomechanical profile of 'optimal' transitions for metabolic benefit, informing the design of targeted interventions (e.g., 'loaded' sit-stand protocols) and refining theoretical models of how sedentary behavior impacts health.
proposals.novelty: This integrates high-fidelity biomechanical modeling from ergonomics and sports science into diabetes behavioral epidemiology, a novel synthesis. It shifts the focus from the *absence* of movement (sitting time) to the *mechanical characteristics* of the movements that interrupt it, a previously unexplored dimension in the sitting-glycemia relationship.

proposals.title: Micro-Patterns of Muscular Silence and Activity: High-Frequency Characterization of Sub-Bout Postural Shifts and Their Association with Postprandial Glycemia in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Owen and others established that prolonged, uninterrupted sitting is detrimentally associated with cardiometabolic risk, including glucose and insulin dysregulation. However, the role of micro-patterns of movement *within* sitting bouts—such as fidgeting, shifting, and minor postural adjustments—remains poorly characterized. Understanding these high-resolution behavioral phenotypes is crucial for developing more precise and effective public health guidelines to break up sedentary time.
proposals.hypothesis: That greater frequency and amplitude of sub-bout postural shifts and muscle activations during prolonged sitting, as measured by high-frequency wearable electromyography (EMG) and accelerometry, will be associated with attenuated postprandial glucose and insulin responses in adults with type 2 diabetes.
proposals.method: We will recruit 50 adults with type 2 diabetes for a controlled laboratory study. Participants will wear high-density, wireless EMG sensors on major leg and trunk muscles (e.g., vastus lateralis, erector spinae) and a high-frequency (≥100 Hz) triaxial accelerometer on the thigh during a 4-hour standardized sitting period with a mixed-meal challenge. We will apply machine learning architectures, specifically a convolutional neural network (CNN), to the raw sensor data to automatically segment and classify micro-movement patterns (e.g., leg crosses, weight shifts, trunk repositions). The extracted features (frequency, duration, intensity) will be used as predictors in linear mixed models with continuous glucose monitor (CGM) and serially sampled insulin as outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, physiologically grounded taxonomy of 'active sitting' micro-behaviors. It will quantify their independent association with acute glycemic control, moving beyond crude bout-length metrics. The findings will inform the development of targeted, feasible 'movement snacks' designed to mimic the most metabolically beneficial sub-bout patterns.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses actigraphy to define sedentary bouts, this proposal drills down to the muscular and kinematic events *within* those bouts using multi-modal high-frequency sensing, a scale of analysis not yet applied in diabetes research. It directly extends Owen's 'Silence of the Muscles' concept by characterizing the 'conversation' within the silence.

proposals.title: Pain as a Determinant of Sedentary Time: Using Wearable Electromyography and Actigraphy to Model the Causal Pathway from Musculoskeletal Pain to Activity Behavior
proposals.motivation: Chronic pain is a major barrier to physical activity, but the real-time, dynamic relationship between pain and subsequent movement behavior is poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to design effective, personalized interventions for conditions like type 2 diabetes, where breaking up sedentary time is crucial for glycemic control. Pre-2026, research has established the harms of prolonged sitting and the benefits of activity, but has not modeled pain as a direct, time-varying predictor of behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: That continuous, objective measures of musculoskeletal pain (via wearable electromyography) will significantly predict subsequent short-term (e.g., 30-minute) transitions between sitting, standing, and stepping behaviors, with higher pain levels predicting increased sedentary time and reduced light-intensity activity.
proposals.method: We will recruit 50 adults with chronic lower back pain and elevated type 2 diabetes risk. Participants will wear a thigh-mounted accelerometer (activPAL) to classify postures (sitting, standing, stepping) and a multi-sensor wearable EMG system on the lower back to capture continuous muscle activity and pain-related tension. We will use time-lagged, mixed-effects regression models and machine learning (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) to test if EMG-derived pain metrics (e.g., muscle activation amplitude, variability) predict subsequent behavior transitions, controlling for known confounders like sleep quality (from actigraphy) and mood states.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical model of pain as a real-time determinant of sedentary behavior, moving beyond cross-sectional associations. It will generate a novel methodology combining wearable EMG and actigraphy for ecological momentary assessment of the pain-behavior pathway, directly informing the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) that can prompt activity when pain is low.
proposals.novelty: While Owen's prior work links behavior to metabolic outcomes, this inverts the causal direction to model pain as a *predictor* of behavior. It uniquely integrates his recent methods in wearable EMG ('Silence of the Muscles') and actigraphy-based sleep assessment to address a novel, mechanistic question in behavioral epidemiology.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Composition of Sedentary and Active Behaviors for Cognitive Performance: A Biobehavioral Intervention Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, has established that prolonged sitting and poor sleep impair cardiometabolic health (e.g., glucose, insulin). However, workplace and public health guidelines focus primarily on minimizing musculoskeletal pain or disease risk. There is a critical gap in understanding how different compositions of sitting, standing, and stepping throughout the day influence cognitive performance and productivity, a key outcome for occupational health and well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a non-linear, optimal composition of sitting, standing, and stepping bouts that maximizes objective cognitive performance and subjective productivity, and that this optimal mix will be associated with improved prefrontal cortex hemodynamics and lower postprandial glucose variability.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, randomized crossover trial with 50 office workers. Participants will complete four 6-hour conditions with different, strictly controlled compositions of sitting, standing, and light stepping (walking). Cognitive performance will be assessed hourly using a validated battery (CANTAB). Continuous measures will include prefrontal cortex oxygenation via functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), interstitial glucose via continuous glucose monitors (CGM), and actigraphy. We will use non-linear mixed-effects modeling and response surface methodology to identify the optimal behavioral composition that maximizes cognitive outcomes, treating glucose and hemodynamic measures as potential mediators.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond a 'sit less, move more' paradigm by providing an evidence-based, quantifiable prescription for a daily behavioral composition that enhances cognitive function. It will integrate physiological (glucose, brain hemodynamics) and behavioral (activity patterns) pathways, offering a novel framework for designing productivity-focused workplace interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links activity to health, this study explicitly seeks to *optimize* a behavioral mix for cognitive performance, a positive outcome, rather than just mitigate a negative one. It uniquely combines fNIRS, CGM, and advanced modeling to uncover the dose-response relationship between behavior composition and brain function.

proposals.title: A high-intensity, multi-modal intervention targeting sedentary behavior and sleep to improve glycemic control in high-risk office workers: a randomized controlled trial
proposals.motivation: Sedentary behavior is a major modifiable risk factor for type 2 diabetes, particularly in high-risk individuals with impaired glucose metabolism. While broad-reach workplace interventions exist, they often lack the intensity needed for those at greatest risk. Pre-2026 evidence shows that both prolonged sitting and poor sleep quality independently disrupt glucose homeostasis, suggesting a combined intervention may yield greater metabolic benefit.
proposals.hypothesis: A high-intensity, multi-modal intervention combining frequent, personalized activity breaks with sleep hygiene support will lead to significantly greater improvements in postprandial glucose and insulin sensitivity in high-risk, sedentary office workers, compared to a standard, low-intensity intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month, two-arm RCT in adults with prediabetes or early-stage type 2 diabetes. The high-intensity arm will receive: 1) a wearable suite (actigraphy + continuous glucose monitor) providing real-time, machine learning-driven prompts for posture changes and walking based on individual glucose patterns and sedentary bout duration; 2) thrice-weekly health coaching via video call; and 3) a sleep module using actigraphy-derived metrics to deliver personalized sleep feedback. The control arm will receive a standard package of educational materials and a sit-stand desk. Primary outcomes are continuous glucose monitor-derived measures of glycemic variability and insulin sensitivity (Matsuda index) from an oral glucose tolerance test.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first efficacy data on a scalable, high-intensity intervention that dynamically integrates real-time sedentary and sleep behavior modification with glucose monitoring. It will advance the field by demonstrating whether a precision public health approach, targeting intervention intensity to individual risk and physiological response, is superior to one-size-fits-all strategies for improving cardiometabolic health.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, closed-loop integration of wearable-derived sedentary and sleep data with continuous glucose monitoring to personalize intervention timing and content, moving beyond static, time-based activity break reminders to a physiology-informed model.

proposals.title: A Randomized Micro-Trial of Context-Aware Behavioral Nudges to Reduce Post-Meal Sitting and Improve Glycemic Control in Adults with Prediabetes
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting, especially after meals, is a modifiable risk factor for insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes. While public health campaigns have successfully used behavioral economics (e.g., defaults, framing) to influence vaccination or smoking, their systematic, technology-enabled application to disrupt sedentary patterns in high-risk individuals remains under-explored. This gap limits scalable, low-cost interventions that can complement traditional activity promotion.
proposals.hypothesis: That delivering personalized, context-aware behavioral nudges via smartphone, informed by continuous glucose monitor (CGM) and accelerometry data, will be more effective than generic activity prompts at reducing postprandial sitting time and improving glycemic variability in adults with prediabetes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 4-week microrandomized trial with 80 adults with prediabetes. Participants will wear a CGM (e.g., Dexcom G6) and an activPAL thigh-worn accelerometer. A custom smartphone app will use a reinforcement learning algorithm (a contextual bandit) to select, in real-time, one of three nudge types (goal-setting, social comparison, loss aversion) or no nudge, triggered by post-meal inactivity detected by the activPAL. The algorithm personalizes nudge delivery based on context: time of day, current glucose level, and prior nudge responsiveness. Primary outcomes are device-measured post-meal sitting time and CGM-derived glycemic variability (MAGE).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of a machine-learning-optimized, context-sensitive nudge system for sedentary behavior disruption. It will generate novel evidence on which behavioral economic principles are most effective for glycemic management in real-world settings and deliver a scalable intervention framework that integrates continuous physiological and behavioral monitoring.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond static, one-size-fits-all activity prompts by dynamically personalizing behavioral economic strategies based on real-time glucose and activity context, a method not yet applied to postprandial sitting. It directly bridges Owen's work on 'silent' muscle inactivity and glucose with emerging digital methods for just-in-time adaptive interventions.

proposals.title: Causal Disentanglement of the Physical Activity-Sedentary Time Relationship: A Triangulation Analysis of Non-Linearity and Directionality in Cardiometabolic Risk
proposals.motivation: The association between physical activity (PA) and sedentary time is a cornerstone of public health guidelines, yet its causal nature remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 evidence suggests these behaviors may be non-linearly related and reciprocally influence each other, but conventional observational studies cannot rule out confounding or establish direction. Clarifying this causal structure is critical for designing effective interventions targeting either PA, sitting, or both to improve glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between device-measured moderate-to-vigorous PA (MVPA) and sedentary time is causal, bidirectional, and characterized by non-linear, threshold-based effects at the within-person level, with changes in one behavior disproportionately affecting the other beyond a certain intensity or duration.
proposals.method: We will employ a causal triangulation framework using two pre-2026 data sources. First, we will apply fixed-effects regression and instrumental variable analysis (using neighborhood walkability as an instrument) to large cohort data (e.g., UK Biobank) to estimate between-person causal effects. Second, we will collect intensive longitudinal data via thigh-worn accelerometers (activPAL) and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) on mood and context over 14 days in adults with prediabetes. We will analyze this data using vector autoregressive (VAR) modeling and piecewise regression to test for time-lagged, within-person reciprocity and identify potential inflection points in the PA-sedentary relationship.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated evidence on the causal direction and shape of the PA-sedentary relationship, moving beyond association. It will directly inform whether public health strategies should focus on increasing PA, breaking up sitting, or both, and identify potential critical thresholds for intervention efficacy. The findings will advance methodological rigor in behavioral epidemiology by demonstrating a triangulation approach for causal inference.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of population-level causal inference methods with intensive within-person, time-series modeling to simultaneously address confounding and directionality, while formally testing for non-linear dynamics—a question highlighted but not resolved in pre-2026 literature on sitting and type 2 diabetes.

proposals.title: Residual Cardiometabolic Toxicity: Testing the Incomplete Neutralization of High Sedentary Time by High Physical Activity
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological evidence suggests high physical activity (PA) may attenuate, but not fully eliminate, the mortality risk associated with prolonged sitting. This creates a critical public health and clinical ambiguity: can we confidently advise that 'exercise cancels out sitting'? Pre-2026 research, including our work on sitting, glucose, and insulin, has primarily examined mortality or single-disease endpoints, leaving the biological pathway—whether sitting leaves a residual harmful signal—largely unexamined in high-PA individuals.
proposals.hypothesis: In adults meeting or exceeding high-volume PA guidelines, higher levels of sedentary time will be associated with a dose-response gradient of residual cardiometabolic dysfunction, specifically elevated inflammation (hs-CRP, IL-6), poorer lipid profiles (triglycerides, HDL-C), and higher fasting insulin, indicating incomplete biological neutralization.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis of ~500 adults (aged 45-65) with high objectively measured PA (>300 min/week MVPA via 7-day thigh-worn accelerometry). Sedentary time and patterns (bouts >30min) will be derived from the same accelerometry data. Fasting blood samples will assay hs-CRP, IL-6, lipids, glucose, and insulin. Using multiple linear regression, we will model each biomarker as a function of sedentary time, adjusting for PA volume, sleep (from actigraphy), age, sex, and BMI. A significant positive association between sedentary time and adverse biomarker levels will indicate residual toxicity.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond binary risk associations by providing direct, mechanistic evidence on whether the 'antidote' of high-volume PA fully neutralizes sitting's metabolic toxicity. It will inform more precise public health messaging and could identify high-sitting, high-activity individuals as a subgroup needing targeted sedentary behavior intervention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a toxicological dose-response analogy to test for *residual* biological harm within a high-PA cohort, a direct test of the 'offset' hypothesis, and integrating high-resolution accelerometry with a multi-biomarker panel of cardiometabolic pathway dysfunction.

proposals.title: The 'Active Couch Potato' Paradox: Investigating Residual All-Cause Mortality Risk in Highly Active Adults with High Sedentary Time
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence suggests high physical activity (PA) attenuates, but may not eliminate, the mortality risk associated with prolonged sitting. Public health messaging often implies that being highly active fully offsets sedentary harm, potentially creating a false sense of security. This proposal directly tests this critical assumption to refine guidelines and understand if sitting has intrinsic metabolic harms, independent of PA.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high levels of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) do not fully neutralize the all-cause mortality risk associated with high daily sitting time, indicating a significant residual risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort analysis using device-based data (7-day thigh-worn accelerometry/ inclinometry) from ~10,000 adults (e.g., UK Biobank). Participants will be stratified into high/low sitting time and high/low MVPA groups. The primary analysis will use Cox proportional hazards models to test for an interaction between sitting time and MVPA on all-cause mortality, adjusting for sleep, demographics, and health conditions. A key analytical focus will be testing for a non-zero hazard ratio in the 'High PA/High Sit' group versus the 'High PA/Low Sit' referent.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive, device-based evidence on whether 'offsetting' is complete or partial, directly informing public health messaging. It will advance the theoretical understanding of sedentary physiology by probing if sitting's harms operate through pathways not fully reversed by PA.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shows attenuation, this study explicitly tests the null hypothesis of full risk neutralization in high-PA individuals using precise, device-measured exposure data, moving beyond self-report and composite risk scores.

proposals.title: The PASTBI Index as a Moderator of Intervention Efficacy: A Prospective Validation Using Device-Measured Sedentary and Active Behavior in a Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: The Physical Activity and Sedentary Behavior Intentions (PASTBI) index, derived from wearable EMG and actigraphy, shows promise as a correlate of habitual activity. However, its sensitivity to change and its value in identifying individuals most likely to modify their behavior in response to an intervention is unknown. Validating PASTBI as a moderator of intervention efficacy would transform it from a descriptive tool into a predictive one, enabling targeted, cost-effective behavioral programs for populations at high risk of type 2 diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: Baseline PASTBI scores will moderate the effect of a theory-based physical activity intervention on device-measured changes in sitting time and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA), such that individuals with higher baseline PASTBI (indicating stronger latent behavioral intention) will exhibit greater objectively measured behavioral improvement.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 adults with sedentary lifestyles and elevated type 2 diabetes risk. Baseline assessment includes the PASTBI index (calculated from 7-day thigh-worn accelerometer and EMG data), continuous glucose monitoring, and fasting insulin. Participants are randomized to a 12-week, multi-component intervention (based on Social Cognitive Theory, incorporating sit-to-stand workstations and prompted walking breaks) or a control arm (general health advice). Primary outcomes are device-measured daily sitting time and MVPA, assessed via thigh-worn accelerometers (activPAL) at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months. The primary analysis uses linear mixed models to test the interaction between intervention arm and baseline PASTBI score on behavior change.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for the PASTBI index as a valid moderator of intervention response, moving beyond cross-sectional association. It will establish a methodology for using intention-sensitive biomarkers to personalize behavioral medicine. The findings will directly inform the design of efficient, stratified interventions aimed at improving glucose metabolism through breaking sedentary time.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links PASTBI to habitual behavior, this study tests its novel application as a pre-intervention screening tool to predict *responsiveness* to a behavioral intervention, a critical step for translational precision health. It uniquely integrates device-measured physiology (EMG), behavior (activPAL), and metabolic markers within an RCT framework.

proposals.title: Device-based validation of the Physical Activity and Sedentary Time Behavioural Index (PASTBI) and its association with glycaemic control in adults at risk of type 2 diabetes
proposals.motivation: The PASTBI is a novel composite index that integrates self-reported physical activity and sitting time to quantify overall movement behaviour healthiness. However, its reliance on self-report is a major limitation, as such data are prone to recall and social desirability biases. Validating the PASTBI against objective, device-measured data is a critical next step to establish its utility as a robust tool for epidemiological research and clinical risk assessment in the context of cardiometabolic health, particularly glucose regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the PASTBI score calculated from device-measured activity data will show a stronger, more precise inverse association with markers of glycaemic control (fasting insulin, HOMA-IR, 2-hr glucose) than the self-reported PASTBI. Furthermore, we expect the device-based PASTBI will more effectively discriminate between individuals with optimal and sub-optimal glycaemic profiles.
proposals.method: We will analyse existing data from a cohort of ~300 adults at elevated risk for type 2 diabetes, who have concurrently worn a thigh-worn accelerometer (e.g., ActivPAL) for 7 days and completed the IPAQ-SF. Device data will be processed using validated algorithms (e.g., PALbatch) to derive total sitting time and time in moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA). These objective measures will be used to calculate a device-based PASTBI score, following the original formula. Associations of both self-reported and device-based PASTBI scores with fasting insulin (primary outcome), HOMA-IR, and 2-hr post-load glucose will be tested using multivariable linear regression, adjusting for age, sex, and BMI.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first objective validation of the PASTBI, determining its true measurement properties and clinical relevance. It will clarify whether the index's predictive power for glycaemic outcomes is enhanced by using precise, device-derived behavioural data. The findings will inform future use of the PASTBI in research and potentially in personalised feedback within behavioural interventions.
proposals.novelty: While device-based validation of single behaviours (e.g., sitting) is common, this study innovatively applies this approach to a validated composite index designed to capture the integrated healthiness of the full activity spectrum. It directly tests whether the theoretical advantage of a composite index is realised when measured with high-fidelity tools.

proposals.title: Reallocating the 24-Hour Portfolio: A Compositional Isotopic Substitution Analysis of Sedentary Time, Physical Activity, and Cardiometabolic Biomarkers in Adults at Risk of Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: The 24-hour day is a finite resource where time spent in one behavior (e.g., sitting) necessarily displaces another (e.g., walking or sleep). Pre-2026 research by Owen and others has established independent associations of sedentary time and physical activity with glucose metabolism, but the health effect of *reallocating* time between these behaviors within the fixed-sum day remains inadequately quantified. This gap limits the specificity of public health guidelines for time-use, particularly for populations with elevated type 2 diabetes risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Within the compositional system of the 24-hour day, isotemporal substitution of 30 minutes of device-measured sedentary time with an equal duration of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity will be associated with clinically meaningful improvements in HbA1c and fasting insulin, independent of total sleep time and other confounders.
proposals.method: We will apply compositional data analysis (CoDA) and isotemporal substitution models to existing, pre-2026 accelerometer and biomarker data from a cohort of adults with prediabetes. The 24-hour composition (sleep, sedentary, light PA, MVPA) will be expressed in ilr (isometric log-ratio) coordinates. Using linear regression with ilr coordinates as predictors, we will estimate the effect on HbA1c of theoretically reallocating fixed time increments from one behavior to another, holding the remaining composition constant, as per the 'time-use portfolio' analogy. Analyses will adjust for age, sex, BMI, and diet.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide precise, quantitative estimates of the potential cardiometabolic benefit of time reallocation, moving beyond associations of single behaviors. It will directly inform more nuanced behavioral prescriptions by showing which specific substitution (e.g., sitting for standing vs. sitting for walking) yields the greatest glycemic improvement. The application of a formal portfolio substitution framework offers a novel paradigm for conceptualizing and analyzing 24-hour movement guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While isotemporal substitution models exist, their explicit conceptualization through the lens of financial portfolio theory—treating time as a fixed-sum portfolio of 'assets' with different health 'returns'—is novel. This provides a powerful economic analogy to communicate the constrained optimization problem of daily time-use to both scientific and policy audiences, grounding the statistical model in an intuitive theoretical framework.

proposals.title: Reallocating the 24-hour day: A compositional data analysis of sedentary behavior, sleep, and physical activity in relation to glucose metabolism in adults at risk of type 2 diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, has established that excessive sitting and poor sleep independently impair glucose metabolism. However, time-use behaviors are co-dependent parts of a 24-hour composition; increasing one behavior necessitates decreasing another. Traditional analyses treat these behaviors as independent, failing to capture the integrated nature of daily time use and its metabolic consequences. This limits the development of holistic, time-based lifestyle prescriptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the compositional balance of time spent in sleep, sedentary behavior, light-intensity activity, and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) is significantly associated with fasting insulin and postprandial glucose responses, and that reallocating time from sedentary behavior to sleep or MVPA will be associated with improved glycemic profiles.
proposals.method: We will analyze 7-day thigh-worn accelerometer data (activPAL) from an existing cohort of adults with prediabetes (n~250). The 24-hour composition will be defined as four parts: sleep, sedentary time, light activity, and MVPA. Using CoDA methods, we will employ isometric log-ratio (ilr) coordinates to represent the composition in regression models with fasting insulin and 2-hour glucose as outcomes. We will conduct isotemporal substitution analyses using the `compositions` package in R to model the predicted change in outcomes when fixed durations of time are reallocated from one behavior to another, while holding the remaining composition constant.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence quantifying the glycemic impact of theoretically feasible, whole-day time reallocations in an at-risk population. It will move the field beyond single-behavior recommendations towards integrated, time-based guidelines. The findings will directly inform the design of behavioral interventions that consider the 24-hour cycle as a finite system to be optimized.
proposals.novelty: While CoDA has been applied to physical activity epidemiology, its application to integrate sleep with activity and sedentary behaviors in the context of glucose metabolism is novel. This approach explicitly addresses the co-dependency of behaviors, offering a more rigorous and holistic analytical framework than the standard multivariate regression models used in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Real-time dynamics of the activity-sitting balance: An ecological momentary assessment and wearable sensor study of within-day glucose regulation in adults with type 2 diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, establishes that prolonged sitting and poor sleep impair glycemic control, while light activity like walking is beneficial. However, traditional methods (e.g., daily averages) obscure the dynamic, within-day interplay between sitting, activity, and metabolic state. Understanding these real-time dynamics is crucial for designing effective, personalized just-in-time interventions to improve glucose management.
proposals.hypothesis: That greater temporal fragmentation of sitting time, captured via ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and wearable sensors, will be associated with more favorable postprandial glucose and insulin responses within the same day, and that these associations will be moderated by preceding sleep quality.
proposals.method: We will recruit 80 adults with type 2 diabetes. Participants will wear a continuous glucose monitor (CGM), thigh-worn accelerometer (e.g., activPAL), and wrist-worn actigraphy/sleep monitor for 7 days. Smartphone-based EMA will prompt participants 6 times daily to report current activity context, mood, and fatigue. We will use time-synchronized data streams to apply multilevel vector autoregressive models, analyzing how sequences of sitting bouts, walking breaks (from activPAL), and self-reported context (EMA) predict subsequent CGM glucose excursions and insulin resistance indices (HOMA2-IR) calculated from fasting samples on three mornings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, high-temporal-resolution map of behavioral, psychological, and glycemic dynamics in free-living adults with type 2 diabetes. It will move beyond aggregate associations to identify critical behavioral sequences (e.g., prolonged sitting after poor sleep) that acutely worsen glycemia, offering concrete targets for micro-interventions.
proposals.novelty: While EMA is established in psychology and sensor-based activity measurement in health, their synchronous fusion to model *real-time, within-day* causal pathways between behavior, context, and glucose is novel. This approach treats the activity-sitting balance as a dynamic, moment-to-day process rather than a static exposure.

proposals.title: Validated Activity-Sedentary Transition Patterns and Next-Day Glycemic Control: A Multi-Sensor Fusion Study in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence links frequent breaks in sedentary time to improved cardiometabolic health, but accelerometry-based transition counts are confounded by trivial movements and measurement noise, creating a poor signal-to-noise ratio. This obscures whether specific, physiologically meaningful patterns of transitioning from sitting to activity are uniquely linked to acute biological pathways. A method to filter and characterize validated transitions is needed to understand real-world micro-patterning.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific temporal patterns of validated, metabolically relevant activity-sedentary transitions (e.g., clustered postprandial transitions, prolonged sitting punctuated by vigorous breaks) are associated with improved next-day markers of glycemic variability and endothelial function, independent of total activity volume.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adults at risk for type 2 diabetes. Participants will wear a multi-sensor device (triaxial accelerometer + continuous heart rate monitor) for 7 days. We will develop an event-filtering algorithm using heart rate elevation (e.g., >10% above sitting baseline) and accelerometry (e.g., >100 mg) to identify 'validated transitions' from noise. Validated events will be abstracted into temporal patterns using unsupervised learning (e.g., k-means clustering on timing, intensity, and context). In an embedded mechanistic sub-study (n=40), continuous glucose monitors and morning endothelial function (via peripheral arterial tonometry) will test associations with next-day physiology.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first evidence linking objectively defined, real-world patterns of activity-sedentary transitions to acute cardiometabolic pathways. It will deliver a novel, validated method for filtering transition events in free-living research, moving the field beyond simple frequency counts toward a mechanistic understanding of behavioral patterning.
proposals.novelty: The approach fuses heart rate and accelerometry to define and filter a 'validated transition' as a discrete physiological event, overcoming a key measurement flaw. It then tests the acute, next-day mechanistic relevance of derived temporal patterns, a direct link not previously established.

proposals.title: The Spectral Density of Sedentary Fragmentation: A Novel Signal-Processing Metric for Predicting Glycemic Dysregulation in Adults at Risk of Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, shows that breaking up sitting time improves glucose and insulin metabolism. However, current metrics like total sedentary time or simple bout frequency lack the resolution to characterize the complex temporal patterning of activity-sitting transitions. A high-resolution metric is needed to understand if specific 'rhythms' of inactivity fragmentation are more metabolically beneficial, moving beyond 'more breaks are better' to 'what pattern of breaks is optimal'.
proposals.hypothesis: The spectral density profile of activity-sitting transitions, derived from minute-by-minute accelerometry, will reveal distinct frequency signatures. We hypothesize that a greater power in higher frequency bands (indicating more frequent, fine-grained fragmentation) will be independently associated with more favorable postprandial glucose and insulin responses, after accounting for total sedentary time and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing 7-day thigh-worn accelerometry data (e.g., from ActivPAL devices) from adults with prediabetes. The binary sit/stand+walk time-series will be treated as a discrete signal. We will apply Welch's method for power spectral density (PSD) estimation, using a Hamming window and appropriate segment overlap to compute the signal's power distribution across frequency bands (e.g., cycles per hour). Key PSD-derived metrics (e.g., spectral centroid, power in 0.5-2 cycle/hour band) will be extracted. These novel metrics will be used as predictors in linear regression models, with outcomes from standardized oral glucose tolerance tests (2-h glucose, insulin AUC).
proposals.contribution: This study will introduce a fundamentally new analytical framework from signal processing to physical behavior epidemiology. It will provide the first evidence linking the spectral characteristics of sedentary fragmentation to direct metabolic outcomes. The resulting metric could become a standardized tool for quantifying the 'quality' of sedentary break patterns in future research and personalized interventions.
proposals.novelty: While actigraphy data is commonly analyzed in the time domain (bouts) or for sleep/wake cycles, applying Fourier analysis to treat activity-sitting transitions as a communicable signal for its frequency 'fingerprint' is a novel conceptual and analytical leap. This moves the field from counting events to characterizing their underlying temporal structure.

proposals.title: The 24-Hour Triad: Developing and Validating a Three-Way Sleep, Sedentary, and Physical Activity Balance Index for Glycemic Control in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, has established that the balance between sedentary time and physical activity is crucial for metabolic health. However, sleep, a core pillar of the 24-hour day, is often analyzed separately. Integrating sleep duration into a holistic three-way behavioral ratio is a logical next step to better understand how the entire daily cycle influences glucose regulation, particularly in high-risk populations like those with Type 2 Diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel three-way balance index, incorporating device-measured sleep duration, sedentary time, and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA), will be more strongly associated with glycemic control (HbA1c, fasting insulin) than traditional two-way (sedentary vs. activity) indices in adults with Type 2 Diabetes.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 adults with Type 2 Diabetes. Participants will wear an activPAL4 thigh-mounted accelerometer and an ActiGraph wGT3X-BT on the wrist for 7 days to objectively measure sitting/standing, stepping (activPAL), and sleep (ActiGraph using the Cole-Kripke algorithm). The primary exposure is a three-way ratio: (Sleep Duration) / (Sedentary Time) / (MVPA Time), log-transformed for analysis. Associations with fasting plasma insulin (primary outcome) and HbA1c will be tested using multivariable linear regression, adjusting for age, sex, BMI, and medication use. We will compare the variance explained (R²) by this model to models using only two-behavior ratios.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first validated metric that quantifies the integrated balance of all three core movement behaviors in relation to diabetes biomarkers. It will advance the field from a dual- to a triadic-behavior paradigm, offering a more comprehensive tool for public health messaging and a stronger predictor for clinical risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: While sleep is recognized as important, its explicit integration into a single, continuous balance metric with sedentary behavior and physical activity is novel. This moves beyond analyzing behaviors in isolation or as simple co-variables, directly testing the hypothesis that their relative proportions within a finite 24-hour period are key.

proposals.title: Causal dose-response curves for physical activity, sitting, and glycemic control: A targeted learning approach to estimate personalized thresholds
proposals.motivation: Current public health guidelines for physical activity and sedentary behavior are based on population averages, which may not be optimal for all subgroups. Observational studies linking these behaviors to cardiometabolic outcomes like glucose and insulin are plagued by time-varying confounding and reverse causality, limiting causal inference. A robust, data-driven method is needed to estimate personalized thresholds that account for complex confounding and effect heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the causal dose-response relationships between physical activity, sitting time, and glycemic outcomes (fasting glucose, insulin resistance) are non-linear and exhibit significant heterogeneity across population subgroups defined by age, sex, BMI, and baseline health conditions.
proposals.method: We will apply Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE) and Double Machine Learning (DML) to longitudinal cohort data with device-measured activity (actigraphy) and sitting (wearable EMG), repeated biomarker measures (glucose, insulin), and extensive time-varying confounders (sleep, diet, mood). TMLE will be used to estimate the average treatment effect of activity/sitting doses, while DML will flexibly model the nuisance parameters. Subgroup-specific causal dose-response curves and threshold points (e.g., where benefit plateaus) will be formally tested within the model framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causally robust, personalized threshold estimates for physical activity and sitting time in relation to glycemic control, with valid confidence intervals. It will advance methodological rigor in behavioral epidemiology by demonstrating how to integrate causal inference and machine learning with device-based measures. The findings will directly inform more precise public health recommendations tailored to specific population groups.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal integration of causal inference (TMLE/DML) with high-resolution behavioral phenotyping (actigraphy, EMG) to estimate personalized thresholds, moving beyond associative models and crude subgroup analyses to explicitly model and test for heterogeneous causal effects.

proposals.title: Personalizing Physical Activity and Sedentary Behavior Thresholds for Glycemic Health: A Pharmacometric Dose-Response Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Current public health guidelines for physical activity (PA) and sedentary behavior (SB) are largely one-size-fits-all, despite evidence of high inter-individual variability in metabolic responses. Pre-2026 research by Owen and others has established clear links between sitting time, activity breaks, and biomarkers like glucose and insulin, but lacks a formal framework to define personalized thresholds. This gap limits the precision of behavioral prescriptions for preventing and managing conditions like type 2 diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between PA/SB 'dose' (exposure) and glycemic 'response' (e.g., fasting glucose, insulin sensitivity) can be robustly characterized using a nonlinear mixed-effects modeling framework, and that key covariates (e.g., age, sex, BMI, comorbidity status) will significantly modify this dose-response curve, enabling the derivation of personalized PASTBI thresholds.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing high-frequency, multi-day data from studies using thigh-worn accelerometers (e.g., activPAL) to measure sitting and activity patterns, paired with fasting or continuous glucose/insulin biomarkers. Using NONMEM or the R package 'nlme', we will fit population pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD)-inspired models. PA/SB metrics (e.g., daily sedentary time, moderate-intensity activity minutes) will be the 'dose', and glycemic outcomes the 'response'. Covariate modeling will formally test and quantify how individual characteristics shift the dose-response relationship, identifying distinct subgroups with different threshold requirements.
