proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven, quantitative framework for generating personalized PA/SB thresholds, moving beyond population averages. It will deliver a novel modeling tool (PASTBI thresholds) that can be tested in intervention studies and inform more precise public health messaging and clinical behavioral prescriptions for metabolic health.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of a mature pharmacometric methodology (nonlinear mixed-effects PK/PD modeling) to the behavioral epidemiology of PA/SB, treating behavior as a 'drug' with a quantifiable dose-response relationship and inter-individual variability, a conceptual and analytical leap beyond standard regression analyses.

proposals.title: Standing Accumulation vs. Sitting Interruption: A Randomized Crossover Trial Comparing Effects on Postprandial Glucose and Insulin in Adults with Overweight or Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, has established that prolonged sitting impairs glycemic control and that breaking up sitting with brief activity bouts is beneficial. However, the specific metabolic impact of accumulating sustained standing time—a feasible, low-intensity alternative—remains unclear. This study directly addresses whether a behavioral focus on building longer standing bouts offers comparable or superior metabolic benefits to the established paradigm of frequent sitting interruptions, which can be disruptive.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a condition prioritizing the accumulation of sustained standing bouts (e.g., 20-30 minutes) will lead to greater improvements in postprandial glucose and insulin responses compared to a condition focused solely on interrupting sitting with frequent, short-duration activity breaks, in adults at risk for or with Type 2 diabetes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, randomized crossover trial. Participants (n=30, adults with overweight/obesity or T2D) will complete three 7-hour conditions on separate days: 1) prolonged sitting (control), 2) sitting interrupted with 3-minute light walking every 30 minutes, and 3) sustained standing, involving two 30-minute standing bouts post-meals. Continuous glucose monitors and serial blood draws will assess glycemic and insulinemic responses. Wearable thigh-mounted accelerometers (activPAL) will objectively quantify sitting and standing time. The primary analysis will compare incremental areas under the curve (iAUC) for glucose and insulin across conditions using linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental evidence comparing the glycemic efficacy of two distinct behavioral targets: sitting interruption versus standing accumulation. It will clarify whether public health and clinical messaging should simply encourage 'moving more' or specifically promote 'standing longer.' The findings will inform more precise and practical guidelines for reducing cardiometabolic risk in office and home environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied standing as a break from sitting, this inverts the focus to treat sustained standing as the primary behavioral goal, a paradigm shift from interruption to accumulation. It leverages precise, device-based measurement of posture (via activPAL) to test this novel behavioral prescription against the established 'break up sitting' paradigm.

proposals.title: The 'Unstructured' Sit-Less Intervention: A Randomized Trial Testing the Cardiometabolic Effects of Sitting Reduction Without Prescribed Light Activity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, demonstrates that breaking up prolonged sitting with light activity improves postprandial glucose and insulin. However, interventions typically prescribe specific, structured activities (e.g., 3 minutes of walking every 30 minutes). It remains unknown whether the health benefits arise from the reduction in sitting time itself or are contingent on the replacement behavior, a critical distinction for designing feasible public health guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reducing total sitting time, even without prescribing a structured light-activity replacement, will yield significant improvements in 24-hour glucose and insulin profiles in adults with overweight/obesity, but that these improvements will be attenuated compared to a matched-duration, structured light-activity intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, three-arm laboratory crossover trial (Sit-Less-Unstructured, Sit-Less-Structured, Control) with 45 adults at risk for type 2 diabetes. Continuous glucose monitors and fasting/2-hour postprandial insulin will be primary outcomes. The 'Unstructured' arm will use a thigh-worn accelerometer (activPAL) linked to a smartphone app providing real-time, haptic feedback on sitting bouts >30 minutes, prompting a posture change without specifying activity. The 'Structured' arm will use the same system but prescribe 3 minutes of light walking. Machine learning algorithms (random forest classifiers) applied to the activPAL and wrist-worn ActiGraph data will objectively verify intervention fidelity and quantify the spontaneous activity profile (postural shifts, standing, incidental movement) in the Unstructured arm.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the mechanistic assumption that structured activity is necessary for sitting-reduction benefits, informing more flexible intervention designs. It will provide novel data on the 'natural' movement ecology that replaces unstructured sitting reduction, using advanced actigraphy and machine learning methods from Owen's recent sleep and EMG work.
proposals.novelty: While many studies replace sitting with prescribed activity, no pre-2026 trial has isolated the effect of sitting reduction *per se* using real-time biofeedback without an activity prescription, nor used machine learning to characterize the resulting spontaneous movement patterns.

proposals.title: A randomised controlled trial of a wearable-based sedentary interruption intervention for glycaemic control in individuals with established type 2 diabetes
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a robust risk factor for poor glycaemic control and cardiovascular disease, with effects acutely mediated by reduced skeletal muscle glucose uptake. While interventions to break sitting time have shown promise in at-risk and older populations, their efficacy in individuals already diagnosed with type 2 diabetes (T2D) remains understudied. This is a critical gap, as this population has impaired insulin action and stands to benefit substantially from strategies that improve 24-hour glucose profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that a technology-supported intervention to regularly interrupt prolonged sitting with brief bouts of light-intensity activity will lead to greater improvements in 24-hour mean glucose levels and postprandial glucose excursions, compared to a sitting-as-usual control, in adults with T2D.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 4-week, two-arm parallel randomised controlled trial in 60 adults with T2D. The intervention group will use a thigh-worn accelerometer (activPAL) linked to a smartphone app that prompts 3-minute bouts of light walking every 30 minutes of sitting during waking hours. The control group will maintain habitual behaviour. The primary outcome is 24-hour mean glucose measured via continuous glucose monitor (CGM) in the final week. Secondary outcomes include CGM-derived glycaemic variability, fasting insulin, and actigraphy-measured sleep. We will use linear mixed models to analyse between-group differences, adjusting for baseline values.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence on the efficacy of a scalable, real-world sedentary interruption intervention specifically for glycaemic management in T2D. It will extend Owen's previous work beyond at-risk groups to a clinical population, informing non-pharmacological management strategies. Findings will also elucidate whether improvements in glucose metabolism are accompanied by changes in sleep quality, integrating two key behavioural domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on prevention in at-risk groups, this trial directly targets the pathophysiological core of an established disease (T2D) with a precise, wearable-driven intervention. It uniquely integrates high-resolution CGM, activPAL-derived sitting metrics, and actigraphy-based sleep assessment to provide a holistic view of 24-hour behavioural and metabolic dynamics.

proposals.title: Machine Learning-Derived Sedentary Phenotypes from Accelerometry Predict Postprandial Glycemic Dysregulation in Adults at Risk for Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Prolonged, uninterrupted sitting is an independent risk factor for type 2 diabetes (T2D), but not all sedentary patterns are equally harmful. Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, shows that breaking up sitting improves glucose and insulin, yet public health guidelines lack specificity on *how* to break it up effectively. Current accelerometer metrics (e.g., total sedentary time, breaks) are simplistic and fail to capture the complex temporal patterns of sitting and light activity that may be most metabolically detrimental.
proposals.hypothesis: Unsupervised machine learning applied to high-resolution thigh-worn accelerometer data will identify distinct, reproducible sedentary behavior phenotypes, and these phenotypes will be differentially associated with postprandial glucose and insulin responses, independent of total sedentary time and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.
proposals.method: We will analyze 7-day, 24-hour raw accelerometer data (ActivPAL) from ~500 adults at high risk for T2D. Using time-series feature extraction (e.g., catch22 features) and unsupervised learning (Gaussian Mixture Models, hierarchical clustering), we will derive phenotypes based on patterns of bout duration, fragmentation, and temporal distribution across the day. These phenotypes will be validated against gold-standard measures of glycemia (2-hour oral glucose tolerance test, continuous glucose monitoring-derived metrics) using mixed-effects regression, adjusting for confounders including sleep metrics (from actigraphy).
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond aggregate measures to provide a data-driven taxonomy of 'high-risk' sedentary behavior, offering a novel framework for precision public health. It will generate specific, phenotype-targeted intervention hypotheses (e.g., targeting morning prolonged sitting vs. evening fragmented sitting) to improve the efficacy of diabetes prevention programs.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been used for activity recognition, its application to *derive* metabolically relevant sedentary phenotypes from raw accelerometry is novel. This approach integrates Owen's expertise in sedentary behavior, glucose metabolism, and emerging wearable sensing (fNIRS, EMG) by applying pattern recognition to a core, population-relevant signal.

proposals.title: The Acute Glycemic and Insulinemic Impact of Micro-Breaks: A Randomized Crossover Trial Comparing Seconds-Long Standing Interruptions to Standard 2-Minute Breaks
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a recognized risk factor for impaired glucose metabolism and type 2 diabetes. While breaking up sitting with light activity (e.g., 2-5 minute walks) improves postprandial glucose and insulin, such breaks are often not adopted in real-world office settings due to practical constraints. The minimal effective dose of interruption remains unknown, creating a critical gap for feasible public health guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that very brief, seconds-long standing interruptions ('micro-breaks') will elicit a detectable, though attenuated, acute improvement in postprandial glucose and insulin compared to longer 2-minute standing breaks, and that both will be superior to uninterrupted sitting.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, three-condition randomized crossover trial with adults at risk for type 2 diabetes. Conditions are: 1) uninterrupted sitting, 2) 2-minute standing breaks every 30 minutes, and 3) 10-second standing micro-breaks every 10 minutes. During a 7-hour protocol with standardized meals, we will measure interstitial glucose via continuous glucose monitors and serum insulin via frequent blood sampling. Primary outcomes are incremental area under the curve for glucose and insulin. We will use mixed-effects models to compare conditions, adjusting for period and sequence effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish the physiological lower bound for effective sedentary interruption, directly informing the 'minimum dose' for guideline development. It will provide novel mechanistic insight into whether frequent, negligible-effort postural shifts can meaningfully influence metabolic regulation, bridging the gap between laboratory efficacy and real-world feasibility.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines breaks of minutes, this is the first to systematically test the efficacy of interruptions lasting only seconds, a radically downsized yet potentially more adoptable intervention. It uniquely applies high-frequency metabolic monitoring to a behavioral paradigm of ultra-brief duration.

proposals.title: Long-term durability of a sedentary behavior intervention on cardiometabolic health: A 24-month follow-up of the Silence of the Muscles trial
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence shows that reducing and breaking up sitting time improves postprandial glucose and insulin in the short term, particularly for those with or at risk of type 2 diabetes. However, the critical public health question is whether these acute cardiometabolic benefits translate into sustained improvements over years, which remains unknown. Determining the long-term durability of behavioral interventions is essential for understanding their real-world impact on chronic disease prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the significant improvements in postprandial glucose, insulin, and vascular function observed immediately post-intervention and at 12-week follow-up will be attenuated but still detectable at 24-month follow-up. Furthermore, we hypothesize that greater maintenance of reduced sedentary time and increased light-intensity activity will be associated with better preservation of cardiometabolic benefits.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month follow-up of participants from the original 'Silence of the Muscles' randomized controlled trial. Participants will wear a thigh-worn activPAL4 accelerometer for 7 days to objectively measure sitting, standing, and stepping time. Cardiometabolic outcomes will be assessed via a standardized 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with frequent blood sampling for glucose and insulin, and via flow-mediated dilation (FMD) to measure endothelial function. We will use linear mixed models to compare changes from baseline, 12-week, and 24-month timepoints between intervention and control groups, adjusting for key covariates. Actigraphy-derived sleep metrics will be included as a covariate given their known association with glycemia.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term evidence on the durability of a specific sedentary behavior intervention on core cardiometabolic pathways. It will inform clinical and public health guidelines on the necessary frequency and design of 'booster' interventions to sustain benefits. The findings will bridge the gap between short-term efficacy and long-term effectiveness for behavioral diabetes prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While short-term follow-ups exist, a 24-month objective assessment of both behavior and mechanistic cardiometabolic outcomes following a rigorously measured sitting intervention is novel. Integrating wearable electromyography (EMG)-informed sedentary patterns with OGTT and vascular function over this duration moves beyond association to test causal durability.

proposals.title: A Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention to Enhance Adherence to a Multicomponent Activity Program in Hospitalized Adults at Risk of Type 2 Diabetes: A Feasibility Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Hospitalization is a critical period of extreme sedentariness and metabolic dysregulation, particularly for patients with or at risk of type 2 diabetes. While multicomponent activity programs (integrating sitting reduction, light walking, and sleep hygiene) show promise for improving glycemia, adherence during and after the hospital stay is notoriously poor, undermining intervention efficacy. Pre-2026, there is a lack of evidence on how to optimize the delivery of such programs using real-time, personalized strategies that account for fluctuating patient states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI), which delivers personalized prompts for activity, sitting breaks, and sleep timing based on continuous wearables data and ecological momentary assessment of mood and fatigue, will result in significantly greater adherence to a multicomponent activity program during hospitalization and the 4-week post-discharge period, compared to a standard, fixed-schedule intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a feasibility RCT (n=60) in a hospital metabolic ward. All participants will receive the core multicomponent program. The intervention group will receive prompts via a smartphone app driven by a JITAI algorithm. This algorithm will integrate real-time data streams from a wrist-worn device (actigraphy for sleep/wake, 3-axis accelerometry for posture and stepping) and brief smartphone surveys (mood, fatigue). Using pre-2026 machine learning methods (e.g., a random forest classifier trained on pilot data), the system will identify opportune moments for intervention delivery (e.g., prompting a walking bout after prolonged sitting when fatigue is reported as low) and adapt goals daily. Adherence will be measured via device data and self-report.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical framework for delivering a personalized, context-aware physical activity intervention in the complex hospital-to-home transition. It will yield critical feasibility data and preliminary effect sizes on adherence and glycemic markers (continuous glucose monitoring). The work will advance the field from static 'one-size-fits-all' prescriptions towards dynamic, responsive behavioral support systems.
proposals.novelty: While JITAIs have been explored in community settings, their application to acutely manage sedentariness, activity, and sleep in hospitalized patients—integrating biometrics with real-time mood states—is novel. This directly extends Owen's work on 'muscle silence' and screen use by targeting the intervention delivery mechanism itself.

proposals.title: A Gamified, Wearable-Based Intervention to Reduce Postprandial Glycemic Excursions by Displacing Sedentary Time with Light-Intensity Activity in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting time is a significant, independent risk factor for poor glycemic control and insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes (T2D). Pre-2026 research by Owen and others shows that interrupting sitting with brief, frequent bouts of light-intensity activity (e.g., walking) acutely improves postprandial glucose and insulin. However, long-term adherence to such behavioral 'sit-less' strategies remains a critical challenge, limiting their real-world impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a smartphone-delivered, behaviorally-gamified intervention (incorporating points, challenges, and real-time feedback) will significantly increase adherence to prescribed sedentary interruptions over a 4-week period, leading to greater reductions in continuous glucose monitor (CGM)-derived postprandial glucose excursions compared to a standard, non-gamified activity prescription.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 4-week, two-arm randomized controlled pilot trial (n=40 adults with T2D). All participants will wear a thigh-mounted accelerometer (activPAL) and a CGM (Dexcom G6). Both groups will receive the same core prescription: interrupt sitting every 30 minutes with 3 minutes of light walking. The intervention arm will access a custom gamified app using a points-based reinforcement algorithm, where adherence earns points, unlocks daily challenges, and maintains 'streaks.' The control arm will use a basic timer app. Primary outcomes are device-measured adherence (proportion of prompted breaks taken) and mean postprandial glucose incremental area under the curve (iAUC). We will use linear mixed models to analyze between-group differences.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first objective evidence on whether gamification can enhance adherence to a physiologically-targeted 'sit-less' strategy in T2D. It will contribute a novel, scalable digital health framework for improving glycemic management by directly targeting sedentary behavior patterns. Findings will inform the design of larger efficacy trials and the development of personalized behavioral nudges in diabetes care.
proposals.novelty: While gamification has been applied to general step counts, its integration with a precise, biomarker-informed prescription to interrupt sitting for glycemic benefit is novel. This proposal uniquely combines Owen's expertise in sedentary physiology and wearable electromyography/actigraphy with emerging digital health methods to solve a core adherence problem.

proposals.title: The Sedentary Reduction Intervention for Vascular Endothelium (STRIVE) Trial: A randomized controlled trial of breaking sitting time on endothelial function in adults with impaired glucose regulation
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a distinct cardiometabolic risk factor, linked to poor glycemic control and vascular dysfunction, independent of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity. While exercise is beneficial, many adults find structured programs difficult to adopt and sustain. It remains unclear whether a singular focus on reducing and breaking up sedentary time, without prescribing exercise, is a sufficient and effective public health strategy for preserving cardiovascular health, specifically endothelial function, a key early marker of atherosclerosis.
proposals.hypothesis: In adults with impaired glucose regulation (prediabetes or early type 2 diabetes), a behavioral intervention targeting the reduction and frequent interruption of sedentary time will lead to significant improvements in flow-mediated dilation (FMD) of the brachial artery, compared to a health education control group, independent of changes in moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month, two-arm randomized controlled trial (n=120). The intervention group will receive a tailored, technology-supported program using thigh-worn activPAL devices and ecological momentary assessment via smartphone to provide real-time feedback and prompts to break sitting every 30 minutes with 3-5 minutes of light-intensity activity (e.g., standing, walking). The control group will receive standard health education materials. The primary outcome is change in ultrasound-measured FMD. Secondary outcomes include 7-day activPAL-derived metrics (sitting time, breaks, standing, stepping), continuous glucose monitoring profiles, fasting insulin, and mood states assessed via fNIRS during controlled sitting tasks. Analysis will use linear mixed models, adjusting for baseline values and MVPA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, causal evidence on whether reducing sedentary behavior alone is sufficient to improve a fundamental measure of cardiovascular function. It will clarify the independent physiological contribution of sedentary reduction, informing more accessible and potentially more sustainable public health and clinical guidelines for diabetes and cardiovascular disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates sitting with cardiometabolic risk, this trial directly tests the sufficiency of sedentary reduction as a standalone intervention for vascular health, integrating high-resolution, objective measurement of behavior (activPAL, CGM) with mechanistic vascular (FMD) and neurovascular (fNIRS) outcomes in an at-risk population.

proposals.title: The Efficacy of Simple Resistance Activities Versus Aerobic Interruptions for Postprandial Glycemic Control in Adults with Sedentary Occupations: A Randomized Crossover Trial
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is an independent risk factor for impaired glycemic control and type 2 diabetes. While breaking sitting with light walking is beneficial, practical constraints in many workplaces limit its feasibility. Simple resistance activities (SRAs), such as chair squats or calf raises, offer a potentially more discreet and space-efficient alternative, but their comparative efficacy for glycemic management is not well-established.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that frequent, brief bouts of simple resistance activities will be superior to equivalent durations of light walking, cycling, or standing breaks in attenuating postprandial glucose and insulin responses in sedentary adults.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, randomized crossover trial with 25 adults at risk for type 2 diabetes. Participants will complete four 7-hour conditions on separate days: prolonged sitting (control), sitting interrupted with 3-minute bouts of light walking every 30 minutes, sitting interrupted with 3-minute bouts of stationary cycling, and sitting interrupted with 3-minute bouts of SRAs (e.g., bodyweight squats, knee lifts). Continuous glucose monitors and serial venous blood samples will measure glycemic and insulinemic responses to standardized meals. Actigraphy and wearable electromyography will quantify muscle activity and sedentary time patterns. Data will be analyzed using linear mixed models with condition as a fixed effect.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, mechanistic comparison of SRA breaks against established activity breaks for glycemic control. It will determine if SRAs, which require minimal space and no equipment, are a viable and potent intervention for mitigating the metabolic harms of sitting in real-world settings, informing practical public health guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares sitting to walking breaks, this study directly tests the novel hypothesis that muscle-loading SRAs may induce superior metabolic benefits compared to aerobic interruptions of equal duration, leveraging precise physiological measurement to move beyond simple activity substitution.

proposals.title: The Dose-Response of Sedentary Breaks: A Randomized Crossover Trial of Break Duration and Frequency on Postprandial Glycemia and Insulinemia
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is an independent risk factor for poor glycemic control and type 2 diabetes. While breaking up sitting with light activity improves postprandial glucose and insulin, the optimal 'dose'—defined by the duration and frequency of breaks—remains unclear. Precisely defining this dose-response relationship is critical for developing efficient, evidence-based public health and clinical guidelines for interrupting sedentary behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there will be a graded, non-linear dose-response relationship, where both longer break durations and higher break frequencies will improve postprandial glycemic and insulinemic responses, but that a threshold exists beyond which additional benefits plateau.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based, randomized crossover trial with adults at elevated risk for type 2 diabetes. Participants will complete five, 7-hour conditions on separate days: prolonged sitting (control) and four intervention conditions systematically varying break duration (2-min vs. 5-min) and frequency (every 30-min vs. every 60-min) of light-intensity walking. Continuous glucose monitoring and serial venous blood sampling will measure glycemic and insulinemic responses to standardized meals. The primary outcome will be the incremental area under the curve (iAUC) for glucose and insulin. Data will be analyzed using linear mixed models to test main and interaction effects of duration and frequency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, experimentally derived dose-response matrix for sedentary break characteristics, moving beyond simple 'breaks vs. no breaks' comparisons. It will directly inform the refinement of physical activity guidelines regarding sedentary interruption and offer practical, optimized parameters for workplace and lifestyle interventions aimed at improving metabolic health.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes the benefit of breaking sitting, this study uniquely employs a factorial design to disentangle and quantify the independent and interactive effects of two key, modifiable dose parameters—duration and frequency—which have not been systematically tested together in a single experiment.

proposals.title: Feasibility and Glycemic Impact of a Technology-Supported, High-Frequency Activity Break Intervention Over Three Months in Adults with Prediabetes
proposals.motivation: Laboratory and acute studies pre-2026 consistently show that interrupting prolonged sitting with light activity improves postprandial glucose and insulin. However, the real-world feasibility and sustained glycemic impact of such interventions over clinically relevant periods (weeks to months) remain poorly understood. This gap is critical for translating a promising behavioral strategy into a scalable, effective public health approach for preventing Type 2 diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a technology-prompted, high-frequency activity break intervention (5-min light walking every 30 minutes) will be feasible with >70% adherence over three months and will lead to clinically meaningful improvements in 24-hour glucose profiles and insulin sensitivity, compared to a standard care control group.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-month randomized controlled feasibility trial in 60 adults with prediabetes. The intervention group will receive a waist-worn ActiGraph accelerometer and a smartwatch that prompts 5-minute light walking breaks every 30 minutes of sedentary time during waking hours. Adherence will be objectively measured via ActiGraph-derived sedentary bout fragmentation and step counts. Primary metabolic outcomes are continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)-derived measures (e.g., mean amplitude of glycemic excursions, time-in-range) and a frequently sampled intravenous glucose tolerance test (FSIVGTT) for insulin sensitivity at baseline and 3 months. We will use linear mixed models to analyze between-group differences.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial evidence on the long-term adherence potential and sustained metabolic efficacy of a high-frequency activity break prescription. It will yield novel data on the dose-response relationship between objectively measured break adherence and 24-hour glycemic control, informing future public health guidelines. The findings will directly address the translational gap between acute laboratory studies and real-world, long-term behavioral interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined acute effects or short-term interventions, this study uniquely combines long-term (3-month) objective monitoring of both behavior (via actigraphy and prompt adherence) and metabolism (via CGM and FSIVGTT) to establish the sustained glycemic impact and practical feasibility of a high-frequency break regimen in an at-risk population.

proposals.title: The Efficacy of One-Minute Micro-Breaks from Sitting on Postprandial Glucose and Insulin Responses in Adults with Overweight or Prediabetes: A Randomized Crossover Trial
proposals.motivation: Prolonged sitting is a robust risk factor for impaired glucose metabolism and type 2 diabetes. While interrupting sitting with short bouts of activity (e.g., 2-5 minutes) improves postprandial glycemia, adherence to such regimens in real-world settings remains a key barrier. Testing the minimal effective dose of activity is crucial for developing feasible, scalable public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interrupting prolonged sitting with one-minute bouts of light-intensity walking every 30 minutes will significantly attenuate postprandial glucose and insulin excursions compared to uninterrupted sitting, and that this regimen will be perceived as more acceptable and easier to adhere to than longer, standard activity breaks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, three-condition crossover laboratory trial in 20 adults with overweight/obesity and elevated cardiometabolic risk. Conditions are: 1) uninterrupted sitting (8h), 2) sitting interrupted with 2-min light walks every 30 min (standard), and 3) sitting interrupted with 1-min light walks every 30 min (micro-break). Continuous glucose monitors and serial blood draws will assess metabolic responses to standardized meals. Wearable electromyography (EMG) and actigraphy will objectively quantify muscle activity and sedentary patterns. Post-trial surveys will assess perceived acceptability and feasibility.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence on the metabolic efficacy of one-minute activity breaks, potentially redefining the minimal physical activity threshold for glycemic benefit. It will also yield critical preliminary data on the perceived adherence and feasibility of an ultra-brief intervention, informing the design of pragmatic, real-world trials.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established benefits for breaks of 2+ minutes, the sufficiency of a one-minute micro-break is unknown. This proposal uniquely integrates objective metabolic, muscular (via EMG), and behavioral adherence measures to test the lower limit of an effective sedentary interruption.

proposals.title: The Stability of Sedentary and Active Behavior Phenotypes: A 12-Month, Multi-Occasion Accelerometry Study to Validate One-Week Sampling
proposals.motivation: Accelerometer-assessed sedentary time and physical activity are critical determinants of cardiometabolic health, including glucose and insulin regulation. The standard 7-day wear protocol is assumed to represent habitual behavior, but its long-term stability across seasons and life contexts is poorly quantified. This gap undermines the validity of etiological studies linking activity patterns to conditions like type 2 diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a one-week accelerometer assessment provides a stable estimate of an individual's habitual sedentary and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) patterns over 12 months, with intra-class correlation coefficients (ICCs) exceeding 0.70, indicating good reliability.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 adults at risk for type 2 diabetes. Participants will wear a thigh-mounted accelerometer (ActivPAL) and a wrist-worn ActiGraph for 7 consecutive days, repeated at 0, 3, 6, 9, and 12 months. We will extract established metrics: daily sitting/sedentary time, standing, stepping (including cadence-based walking bouts), and sleep duration. Stability will be assessed using ICCs (two-way random effects, absolute agreement) for each metric across the five timepoints. We will also use latent class growth analysis to identify subgroups with distinct longitudinal patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, empirical evidence for the validity of the 7-day sampling protocol, strengthening the foundation of future research on sedentary behavior and health. It will directly inform the design of longitudinal and intervention studies in Owen's research themes by quantifying expected within-person variability in key exposure measures.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined week-to-week reliability, this multi-occasion, year-long design with concurrent thigh and wrist wear uniquely captures the stability of 24-hour activity rhythms—integrating sedentary, active, and sleep behaviors—across seasonal and life conditions.

proposals.title: The 'Activity-Sedentary-Sleep' Nexus: A Wearable-Sensor-Guided Intervention to Increase MVPA for Weight Management in Breast Cancer Survivors
proposals.motivation: Breast cancer survivors face elevated risks of weight gain and metabolic dysfunction, including insulin resistance, which negatively impacts long-term health and recurrence risk. Pre-2026 evidence shows that survivors often have low levels of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) and high sedentary time, a combination detrimental to glucose metabolism. Interventions must address the full 24-hour activity cycle, as changes in MVPA can be offset by compensatory increases in sitting or changes in sleep, limiting weight control efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-component intervention that uses real-time wearable sensor feedback to simultaneously increase MVPA and manage sedentary behavior will lead to greater reductions in body weight and improvements in insulin sensitivity in breast cancer survivors, compared to a standard MVPA prescription alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-month randomized controlled trial (n=80). The intervention group will receive: 1) a tailored MVPA goal, 2) a thigh-worn accelerometer (activPAL) providing real-time feedback on sitting bouts via a smartphone app to prompt breaks, and 3) sleep hygiene advice informed by wrist-worn actigraphy (ActiGraph). The control group receives the MVPA goal only. Primary outcomes are weight change and fasting insulin. We will use functional data analysis to model the temporal interplay between MVPA, sedentary bout patterns (from activPAL's validated algorithms), and sleep metrics, testing their association with glycemic outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, integrated framework for weight management in survivors that moves beyond MVPA volume to consider its interaction with sedentary and sleep behaviors. It will generate new evidence on how real-time feedback on sitting can be leveraged to enhance the metabolic benefits of an MVPA intervention, directly addressing compensatory behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While MVPA interventions exist, this integrates Owen's core themes of sitting, activity, and sleep using concurrent, objective monitoring to guide a dynamic behavioral intervention. The use of a thigh-worn sensor for real-time sedentary feedback in an oncology population, combined with actigraphy-based sleep assessment, is a novel application of pre-2026 wearable technology to manage the entire activity cycle for metabolic health.

proposals.title: Geographic Contexts of Sedentary Behavior and Cardiometabolic Risk: A GPS and Wearable Sensor Study in Adults with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Owen and others has established that prolonged sitting and poor sleep are independent risk factors for adverse glucose metabolism. However, the geographic and environmental contexts that shape these behaviors remain poorly understood, limiting targeted public health interventions. Integrating methods from urban health studies with detailed physiological monitoring can reveal how specific locations (e.g., home, workplace, transit) structure activity patterns and metabolic health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that greater time spent in specific geographic clusters (e.g., home) identified via GPS will be associated with longer sedentary bout durations and higher postprandial glucose levels, independent of total sedentary time and sleep quality.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adults with type 2 diabetes. Participants will wear a thigh-mounted accelerometer (activPAL) to measure sitting/standing time, a continuous glucose monitor (CGM), and a GPS logger (e.g., Qstarz BT-Q1000XT) for 7 days. GPS data will be processed using the ST-DBSCAN algorithm to identify significant stationary locations (clusters). We will use time-use diaries to label clusters (home, work, etc.). Multilevel models will test associations between cluster-specific sedentary time (from activPAL) and CGM-derived glycemic metrics (e.g., mean amplitude of glycemic excursions), adjusting for actigraphy-derived sleep measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence linking objectively measured geographic context to real-time glucose variability in a clinical population. It will move beyond aggregate measures of behavior to identify 'risky locations' for prolonged sitting, informing context-aware just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) and urban design policies aimed at diabetes prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used GPS to study physical activity, the novel integration of high-fidelity sedentary bout data (via activPAL EMG/accelerometry), continuous glucose monitoring, and spatial clustering algorithms to dissect location-specific metabolic risk is a significant methodological advance beyond existing epidemiological associations.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of 24-Hour Activity Rhythms: Identifying Time-Use Patterns that Optimally Predict Reductions in Central Adiposity in Survivors of Chronic Conditions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Owen's work, establishes that prolonged sitting and poor sleep impair glucose and insulin regulation, increasing central adiposity—a key risk factor for cardiometabolic disease. While forward models predict health outcomes from activity, a critical gap exists in identifying the specific, time-resolved activity compositions (sleep, sedentary bouts, light activity, walking) that are most protective for reducing waist circumference, especially in high-risk survivor populations where traditional interventions often fail.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an inverse optimization model, trained on high-resolution actigraphy and anthropometric data, will identify non-linear, time-dependent combinations of sedentary fragmentation, walking bout duration/frequency, and sleep quality that are most strongly associated with reductions in central adiposity over time.
proposals.method: We will use data from a longitudinal cohort of survivors (e.g., cancer, diabetes) with pre/post 7-day thigh-worn accelerometry (ActivPAL) and waist circumference. The inverse problem will be framed as a constrained optimization: we will train a multi-task neural network (using TensorFlow 2.x) with a custom loss function that penalizes deviations from observed adiposity reduction. The model inputs are time-use compositions (derived from hidden Markov models on accelerometry), and the output is a predicted change in waist circumference. Gradient-based sensitivity analysis (integrated gradients) will then be applied to the trained model to solve the inverse problem, identifying the input activity patterns that maximize predicted reduction.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a novel, evidence-based 'prescription' for daily activity timing and composition tailored to reducing central adiposity in survivors. It moves beyond generic 'move more, sit less' advice to provide quantifiable, personalized targets for sedentary breaking, walking, and sleep. The method contributes a new analytical framework for deriving behavioral targets from observational data.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard epidemiological paradigm, using machine learning optimization to derive target activity patterns from a desired health outcome, rather than merely associating patterns with outcomes. It integrates Owen's expertise in sedentary behavior, sleep, and glucose metabolism into a single, time-resolved optimization model.

proposals.title: Pre-diagnosis activity patterns as effect modifiers: A longitudinal study of post-diagnosis sedentary behaviour, physical activity, and glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes
proposals.motivation: Research consistently shows that reducing sedentary time and increasing physical activity after a type 2 diabetes (T2D) diagnosis improves glycemic control. However, this relationship may not be uniform; an individual's long-term activity patterns prior to diagnosis could fundamentally alter how they respond to post-diagnosis behaviour change. Understanding this effect modification is crucial for personalizing diabetes management strategies and identifying those who may benefit most from targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between post-diagnosis device-measured sedentary time, light-intensity activity, and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) with HbA1c will be significantly modified by an individual's pre-diagnosis activity pattern, such that the glycemic benefits of post-diagnosis activity will be attenuated in those with a consistently inactive pre-diagnosis history.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal analysis of participants from the UK Biobank and AusDiab studies with incident T2D. Pre-diagnosis activity patterns (high, moderate, low) will be derived from repeated self-reported IPAQ data or, where available, historical accelerometry. Post-diagnosis activity will be measured using 7-day thigh-worn accelerometry (activPAL) to capture sitting, standing, and stepping. Effect modification will be tested using multivariable linear regression models with interaction terms between pre-diagnosis pattern (categorical) and post-diagnosis activity metrics (continuous), adjusting for age, sex, BMI, medication use, and sleep duration (from actigraphy).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence on whether pre-diagnosis lifestyle acts as an effect modifier for post-diagnosis behaviour-health relationships in T2D. It will move the field beyond simple associations to a more nuanced understanding of personal context, directly informing the development of stratified behavioral medicine approaches for diabetes care.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links post-diagnosis activity to health, it treats pre-diagnosis behaviour merely as a confounder. We reconceptualize it as a key modifier, testing a novel life-course hypothesis about response heterogeneity using objective pre- and post-diagnosis data within established cohorts.

ideas.title: Prospectively validate PASTBI's sensitivity to change using device data in a behavioral intervention RCT
ideas.description: This study tests the PASTBI index's validity as a measure of modifiable behavioral intention, not just a correlate of habitual activity. A cohort with sedentary lifestyles is recruited, baseline PASTBI scores are collected, and device-measured activity is established. Participants are then randomized into a theory-based physical activity intervention arm or a control arm. Device-measured activity and sitting time are monitored continuously. The primary analysis tests whether baseline PASTBI scores moderate the intervention's effect on device-measured behavior change, determining if the index identifies individuals whose intentions—and subsequently, their objectively measured activity—are most amenable to change.

ideas.title: Testing the 'Dose-Response Shield': Does High Physical Activity Fully Neutralize the Toxic Dose of Sedentary Time?
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high physical activity (PA) fully offsets the mortality risk of high sitting time by drawing an analogy from toxicology. In that field, to test if an antidote fully neutralizes a poison, scientists administer both the toxin and the antidote in measured doses and then look for residual biological signs of toxicity. Adapting this solution, the research would frame high sitting time as a 'toxic dose' and high-volume PA as the putative 'neutralizing agent.' It would move beyond simple mortality associations to investigate whether, in individuals with high PA, residual cardiometabolic dysfunction (e.g., inflammation, poor lipid profiles) persists in proportion to their sitting time. This approach tests for incomplete 'neutralization' by examining the biological pathway, not just the endpoint, to see if a harmful 'signal' from sitting remains detectable even under a high PA regimen.

ideas.title: Applying Signal Processing to Analyze the 'Spectral Density' of Activity-Sitting Transitions
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from signal processing, where the power spectral density (PSD) analyzes a signal's frequency composition. The analogous solution involves using Fourier transforms to decompose a signal to understand its energy distribution across frequencies. Adapted to the study of minute-by-minute activity-sitting transitions, this approach treats the sequence of transitions (e.g., sitting=0, activity=1) as a binary time-series signal. By calculating its spectral density, researchers can move beyond simple counts to characterize the patterning—quantifying the prevalence of high-frequency 'micropatterns' versus lower-frequency rhythms. This could reveal if specific frequency signatures of inactivity fragmentation are uniquely associated with metabolic, cardiovascular, or cognitive health outcomes, offering a novel, high-resolution metric derived from accelerometry data.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of the PA-Sitting Time Relationship: Testing Non-Linearity and Directionality
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond testing the simple linearity of the PA-sitting time association to rigorously investigate its causal structure and direction. It employs a triangulation of causal inference methods, including longitudinal fixed-effects models and instrumental variable analysis on large-scale cohort data, to control for confounding and estimate causal effects. Simultaneously, it uses intensive longitudinal data from EMA and wearables to model within-person, time-lagged relationships and identify potential thresholds or non-reciprocal dynamics. The integrated design explicitly tests for non-linearity while determining whether the relationship is causal, bidirectional, or spurious, providing a robust evidence base for intervention targeting.

ideas.title: Disentangle TV's health impact using a multi-exposure isolation framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the multi-exposure isolation framework from environmental toxicology, where the health effects of a complex exposure (e.g., living near industry) are partitioned into contributions from specific pollutants, stress, and other factors. The analogous solution involves detailed, concurrent monitoring of each potential exposure pathway. Adapted to the original idea, the study would treat TV viewing not as a monolithic 'sedentary behavior' but as a cluster of co-occurring exposures: postural inactivity, associated caloric intake, and cognitive-emotional stress. By using wearable sensors (accelerometers, continuous glucose monitors, ECG for heart rate variability) and ecological momentary assessment during and after TV sessions, researchers can apply mediation models to statistically isolate and quantify the mechanistic contribution of snacking and mental stress to health outcomes, independent of the inactivity component itself.

ideas.title: Apply Portfolio Substitution Modeling to PASTBI
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from financial economics—specifically, portfolio substitution modeling from portfolio theory—to the PASTBI research context. In finance, the marginal impact of reallocating investment from one asset class to another on a portfolio's risk-return profile is quantified while holding the total investment constant. Analogously, we treat the 24-hour day as a fixed-sum portfolio composed of time-use behaviors (sleep, sedentary time, light physical activity, moderate-to-vigorous physical activity). By applying isotemporal substitution models within a compositional data analysis framework, we can estimate the effect on a health biomarker (e.g., HbA1c, CRP) of substituting a unit of sedentary time with an equal unit of physical activity, precisely isolating the effect of the time reallocation while accounting for the constrained, interdependent nature of the time-use composition.

ideas.title: Causal estimation of personalized PA/sitting thresholds using targeted learning to control for confounding
ideas.description: This study employs a causal inference approach to estimate the effect of physical activity and sitting time on health outcomes across population subgroups. Using targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) and double machine learning on longitudinal data, it models dose-response curves while rigorously adjusting for a wide set of time-varying confounders and baseline characteristics. This method formally tests for heterogeneous treatment effects by pre-specified subgroups within the causal model, reducing the risk of false discovery. The output is a set of robust, causally informed threshold estimates for each subgroup, with valid confidence intervals, suitable for guiding personalized public health recommendations.

ideas.title: Develop personalized PASTBI thresholds using a pharmacometric dose-response modeling framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from clinical pharmacology, where population pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling personalizes drug dosing, to the problem of defining personalized physical activity/sedentary behavior thresholds. The analogous solution involves building mathematical models of dose-response while accounting for patient covariates. The adapted idea treats physical activity and sitting time as the 'dose' and key health biomarkers as the 'response'. Using a nonlinear mixed-effects modeling framework, the research will characterize the population-level PA/SB dose-response relationship and formally quantify how covariates like age, sex, BMI, and comorbidity status modify this relationship, thereby generating data-driven, personalized PASTBI thresholds for distinct subgroups.

ideas.title: Event-Filtered Transition Patterning: Linking Validated Activity Shifts to Acute Physiological Pathways
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple frequency counts to investigate whether specific, physiologically validated patterns of activity-sitting transitions impact short-term health pathways. It uses a multi-sensor fusion (accelerometry + heart rate) to identify and filter true, metabolically relevant transition events from measurement noise and trivial movements. These validated events are then abstracted into temporal patterns (e.g., clustered post-meal transitions, prolonged sitting punctuated by vigorous breaks) whose association with next-day markers of cardiometabolic health (like glycemic variability or endothelial function) is tested in an embedded mechanistic sub-study. This approach directly links real-world micro-patterning to plausible biological mechanisms, overcoming the signal-to-noise ratio fatal flaw of the original design.

ideas.title: National Surveillance of TV-Associated Behavioral Clusters and Cardiometabolic Risk
ideas.description: This research integrates the PASTBI into large-scale national health surveys, but enhances it with supplementary modules that capture the key behavioral and psychological co-exposures (e.g., concurrent snacking, mental stress) hypothesized to drive TV-associated cardiometabolic risk. This creates a national surveillance system that moves beyond monitoring a single behavior to mapping prevalent, real-world behavioral clusters. The resulting data can identify which specific clusters (e.g., TV + stress, TV + high-calorie snacking) are most strongly associated with poor PASTBI scores and adverse health outcomes at the population level, informing more precise and effective public health interventions beyond generic 'move more' messaging.

ideas.title: A Bilevel Optimization Framework for Health Behavior: Minimizing Cost for Target Health Gains
ideas.description: This research synthesizes a health production function (Idea A) with its inverted dual problem (Idea B) into a unified bilevel optimization model. The upper level defines a population or individual's target health gain (e.g., a 20% mortality risk reduction), while the lower level solves for the set of physically active/sedentary behavior combinations that achieve this target at minimal 'cost,' where cost is defined by a behavioral utility function capturing preferences, time, and effort. This framework moves beyond identifying a single optimum to mapping the efficient frontier of behavioral trade-offs for any given health objective, providing a tool to design personalized interventions that are both effective and adherence-aware.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Discover Non-Linear PA-Sitting Interactions
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology—ecological niche modeling—to the problem of discovering non-linear patterns between physical activity (PA) and sedentary behavior (sitting). In ecology, models like MaxEnt use machine learning to find complex, non-linear interactions between environmental variables that define a species' habitat, without pre-specified relationships. We adapt this by framing a positive health outcome as the 'species' and multiple PA/sitting metrics as the 'environment'. Using an ensemble algorithm (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting), the model will learn the high-dimensional, interactive 'niche' for health, revealing how different intensities, durations, and patterns of PA and sitting jointly influence risk, moving beyond simple linear or ratio-based prescriptions.

ideas.title: Modeling TV Mortality Risk as a Dose-Response Curve with Age-Dependent Pharmacokinetics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a constant mortality risk per hour of TV viewing by adapting methodologies from toxicology and pharmacology. The analogous problem is modeling the non-linear dose-response relationship of a substance, where the effect per unit dose changes with total exposure and the recipient's physiological characteristics (e.g., age affecting metabolism). The analogous solution involves using statistical dose-response models that do not assume linearity and that incorporate effect modifiers. Adapted to this context, the research would model TV viewing hours as an exposure dose and mortality as the adverse response. The core method is to fit non-linear models (like splines or threshold models) to large cohort data to identify the shape of the risk curve. Crucially, viewer age is integrated not just as a covariate, but as a potential effect modifier that alters the pharmacokinetics of the 'exposure'—conceptually, how the 'dose' of sedentary behavior and stimulus is 'metabolized' or impacts physiological systems—testing for interaction between age and viewing time to see if risk per hour is higher at older ages.

ideas.title: Cognitive-Architecture-Informed Behavioral Optimization for Physical Activity and Sedentary Time
ideas.description: This research integrates a physiological health production function (modeling PA and sitting as inputs to health outputs) with a validated computational model of human decision-making, such as an instance-based learning or a biased reinforcement learning agent. The combined model simulates how a cognitively realistic agent makes daily activity choices under environmental cues, stress, and fatigue, generating 'attainable behavioral optima' rather than theoretical ones. The output is a set of context-sensitive intervention strategies (e.g., just-in-time prompts, environmental redesigns) designed to steer the biased agent toward the health-optimal region of the production function, explicitly accounting for and overcoming predictable cognitive failures.

ideas.title: Decomposing TV Time: A Signal vs. Noise Approach from Neuroscience
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that TV time is a straightforward proxy for overall sedentarism by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience. In neuroimaging, scientists separate specific neural signals from general background brain activity. Adapting this solution, the research would decompose 'TV time' into a 'specific signal' of unique cognitive-emotional engagement and the 'general noise' of shared physical sedentarism. By employing a within-subjects design comparing TV viewing to other passive leisure activities (e.g., reading, passive music listening) while measuring multi-modal markers (physiological arousal, stress biomarkers, attentional states, and affective response), the study aims to isolate what proportion of TV's associated health risks are attributable to sedentarism itself versus the specific stimulus properties of television, thereby refining its use as an epidemiological proxy.

ideas.title: Applying Dose-Response Modeling from Toxicology to Test the PA-ST Ratio Assumption
ideas.description: This idea challenges the linear, reciprocal ratio assumption between physical activity (PA) and sitting time (ST) by drawing an analogy from toxicology and pharmacology. In those fields, the simplistic linear dose-response assumption was overturned by testing a suite of non-linear models (e.g., sigmoidal, threshold, hormetic) across a wide range of exposures. The analogous solution involved empirical data collection across the full spectrum of doses and rigorous statistical model comparison to identify the true functional relationship. Adapted to this context, the research would treat PA and ST as interacting 'doses' and 'responses,' collecting high-resolution behavioral data across diverse activity levels. Using model comparison techniques (like AIC/BIC), it would test whether the relationship is best described by the assumed linear ratio, or by alternative models such as a threshold (where small PA changes have little effect until a critical point), a saturating curve, or an asymmetric relationship. This would determine if the current ratio-based public health guidance is built on an accurate or an oversimplified biological model.

ideas.title: Deconstructing TV Viewing: A Causal Inference Framework to Isolate Behavioral, Dietary, and Psychological Mechanisms on Health
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond asking if TV's impact is from more than inactivity to actively isolating the distinct causal contributions of its component mechanisms. It integrates longitudinal observational data with genetic epidemiology and controlled behavioral experiments within a formal causal inference framework. This triangulation allows for testing whether snacking and mental stress are independent causal mediators of TV-associated health risks, or merely correlated epiphenomena of the sedentary context. The outcome is a quantitatively assessed pathway analysis, identifying the primary mechanistic targets for effective intervention.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Optimization for Health Behavior Allocation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from financial portfolio optimization to the problem of finding optimal behavioral combinations for health. In the analogous field of financial economics, the Markowitz model finds an optimal asset allocation by solving a constrained optimization that balances expected return against risk, resulting in an efficient frontier of optimal portfolios. We adapt this by modeling physical activity (PA) types and sedentary behaviors as 'health assets' with associated benefits (positive health 'returns') and costs/risks (negative 'volatility'). A health production function is formulated where the output (e.g., cardiometabolic health score) depends on the time 'allocated' to each behavior, with constraints reflecting total available time and physiological limits. The solution involves calculating an 'efficient frontier' of behavior combinations, allowing individuals or clinicians to identify personalized activity portfolios that maximize health utility for a given level of perceived risk or effort, thereby providing a rigorous, quantitative framework for behavioral prescription beyond simple guidelines.

ideas.title: Adapting Geochemical Compositional Analysis to Model 24-Hr Behavioral Compositions
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established solution from geochemistry for analyzing closed compositional data (like rock or sediment samples) to the domain of 24-hour movement behaviors. In geochemistry, CoDA methods, particularly log-ratio transformations, are used to statistically model the relative proportions of components that sum to a constant (e.g., 100% or 24 hours). The adapted research idea treats the 24-hour day as a closed composition, with time spent in sleep, sedentary behavior, light activity, and moderate-to-vigorous physical activity as the components. By applying the same CoDA principles and transformations (like centered or isometric log-ratios) used in geochemistry, we can properly model the data's compositional nature, avoid statistical pitfalls, and interpret results in terms of relative trade-offs between behaviors, rather than isolated effects of each.

ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive subgroup comparisons to test a priori hypotheses about the specific causal pathways (e.g., differential healthcare utilization, variation in comorbid conditions, or distinct patterns of substituting TV time for other activities) that explain heterogeneity in the TV-mortality association. It employs a formal causal mediation analysis design using individual-level cohort data, modeling effect modification by social and environmental factors while adjusting for relevant confounders. The outcome is a mechanistic understanding of risk variation, identifying modifiable intervention targets (like access to recreational facilities or healthcare) for specific high-risk groups, rather than just documenting differences.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery and Falsification Framework for Non-Linear Physical Activity-Sitting Patterns
ideas.description: This research system first employs a causal discovery algorithm to generate a set of plausible causal directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from observational data on physical activity (PA) and sitting. It then uses a constrained neural network, guided by these causal structures, to identify specific non-linear interaction patterns. Crucially, each discovered pattern is treated as a candidate hypothesis and subjected to a pre-registered falsification protocol using methods like negative control exposures or E-values to quantify its robustness to unmeasured confounding, ensuring only robust, interpretable, and potentially causal interactions are reported.

ideas.description: This study uses a sibling-matched cohort design to rigorously control for shared genetic and familial environmental confounders when assessing the link between PASTBI and incident diseases like diabetes and depression. It leverages longitudinal health registries to establish precise temporal order, treating TBI as a time-varying exposure within individuals' life courses. The analysis will adjust for detailed, time-varying measures of socioeconomic status and pre-injury morbidity, and will explicitly test for dose-response relationships to evaluate evidence for a potential causal effect.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map PASTBI's Association with Disease Morbidity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling to study the link between the PASTBI (Physiological Age Score from T1 Bio-impedance) and non-fatal disease morbidity. In ecology, models correlate species occurrence with environmental variables to map habitat suitability and identify key limiting factors. Analogously, we can treat disease incidence (e.g., new diabetes or depression cases) as 'occurrence data' and the multidimensional PASTBI (and its sub-scores for sleep, activity, stress recovery, etc.) as the 'environmental layers.' By applying similar machine learning frameworks (e.g., maximum entropy models or other ensemble methods), the research would aim to create a predictive map of disease risk based on an individual's PASTBI profile. This approach would quantitatively identify which specific physiological domains within the PASTBI score act as the primary limiting or enabling factors for different non-fatal health outcomes, moving beyond simple association to a mechanistic, multi-factorial risk mapping.

ideas.title: Adapting Financial High-Frequency Trading Analytics for Real-Time Behavioral Balance Assessment
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution to a parallel problem in quantitative finance—capturing value from high-frequency, transient market fluctuations—to the challenge of capturing dynamic PA-sitting balance. In finance, High-Frequency Trading (HFT) systems solve this by performing algorithmic analysis on real-time data feeds to identify and act on micro-scale opportunities (like arbitrage) almost instantaneously. We adapt this by treating the continuous stream of ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and sensor data (e.g., from wearables) as the 'market feed' for behavioral states. The adapted research idea involves developing algorithms that perform real-time analytics on this feed to detect micro-patterns signaling a high-probability, contextually feasible moment for a break from sitting (a 'behavioral arbitrage' opportunity). The system would then automatically trigger a personalized, adaptive intervention (e.g., a smartphone prompt or a suggestion integrated into a smart device) in that precise moment, aiming to 'execute' a shift in the activity balance before the sedentary equilibrium re-establishes, thereby optimizing the timing and potential efficacy of just-in-time adaptive interventions.

ideas.title: Equity-Embedded Population Health Surveillance with Disaggregated PASTBI and Disparity Alerts
ideas.description: This system integrates the PASTBI into national health surveys not as a standalone metric, but as the core of a mandatory health equity monitoring protocol. Every population-level PASTBI estimate must be automatically disaggregated and reported across major socioeconomic and demographic strata. An integrated analytics engine continuously monitors these stratified results, generating formal disparity alerts when inequities grow or exceed benchmarks. This design ensures policymakers cannot ignore the distribution of biological aging, transforming the metric from a simple surveillance tool into an engine for equitable health policy and targeted intervention.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Personalization: Mapping Target Risk Reduction to Individualized PASTBI Distributions
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea shifts from finding a single optimal PASTBI value to quantifying the personalized probability distribution of PASTBI configurations that achieve a target mortality risk reduction. It integrates individual-level data into a hierarchical Bayesian model to account for heterogeneity in physiological response. The output provides a range of plausible behavioral combinations tailored to an individual's specific health profile, along with the associated uncertainties. This approach acknowledges biological complexity and offers practical, personalized guidance rather than a one-size-fits-all prescription.

ideas.title: Adapting the Air Quality Index (AQI) Model for National Population Biometric Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from environmental science, where the complex problem of communicating multi-pollutant air quality data to the public and policymakers was solved by creating the standardized Air Quality Index (AQI). Adapting this solution, the research proposes to transform the PASTBI—a multi-system physiological summary metric—into a similarly standardized 'Population Biometric Status Index' (PBSI). This would involve mapping PASTBI scores to a simple, intuitive, color-coded scale (e.g., Green/Yellow/Orange/Red). The PBSI could then be incorporated into national health survey outputs and surveillance dashboards, providing a clear, summary metric for tracking population-level physiological aging and dysregulation trends over time, thereby enabling clearer communication and more responsive public health policy.

ideas.title: Inverse Kinematics for Health: Solving for the PASTBI Dose from a Desired Mortality Risk Reduction
ideas.description: This idea applies the principle of inverse kinematics from robotics to the original seed concept. In robotics, one solves for the necessary inputs (joint angles) to achieve a desired output (end position). Here, we invert the standard epidemiological analysis. Instead of estimating the mortality risk reduction from a given PASTBI level (forward problem), we define a target mortality risk reduction (e.g., a 20% reduction) as the 'desired position.' The analogous 'joint angles' are the components and overall value of the PASTBI. The adapted research idea is to develop a mathematical or computational model—potentially using optimization or root-finding algorithms—that works backwards from the target risk reduction through the established dose-response relationship to solve for the precise PASTBI value associated with that target. This provides a direct translation of population health goals into actionable behavioral targets.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacogenomic Cohort Design for PASTBI Validation Across Demographics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the cohort design solution from pharmacogenomics, where diverse, stratified populations are essential for validating genetic markers, to the validation of the PASTBI (Preschool-Aged Screen for Temperament and Behavior Issues) tool. The analogous solution involves targeted recruitment to build representative cohorts stratified by factors like genetic ancestry and age, followed by analysis to find population-specific effects. Adapted here, the research would establish a multi-site, international consortium to recruit stratified cohorts (e.g., children, older adults, from different countries) not just for replication, but to formally test how demographic and cultural variables moderate the PASTBI's predictive validity. The outcome would be a calibrated validation framework with potential stratum-specific adjustments, ensuring the tool's generalizability and fairness across diverse populations.

ideas.title: Adapting Geologic Fault-Line Stress Analysis to Model PASTBI's Association with Cause-Specific Mortality
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where earthquake risk is modeled by analyzing stress on specific fault segments, to the study of PASTBI (Prior Adverse Symptom or Traumatic Brain Injury) and mortality. The analogous solution involves moving from a general hazard assessment to a segmented, mechanistic projection for specific events. Adapted to this context, the research would investigate the link between PASTBI and cause-specific mortality by segmenting PASTBI exposure into distinct profiles (analogous to fault segments) based on injury characteristics. It would then model the accumulated biological 'stress' from each profile and analyze its association with the 'rupture' of specific fatal pathways, such as cardiovascular disease or cancer, providing a more precise, mechanistic understanding of risk.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Disaggregate Population Health Risk
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of uniform population risk in TV-mortality studies by drawing an analogy from ecological niche modeling. In ecology, species distribution models disaggregate a species' overall range into distinct niches based on heterogeneous environmental data. Adapting this solution, we propose to treat mortality risk analogously to species presence. Socioeconomic, ethnic, and geographic variables serve as the 'environmental layers.' The research would employ niche-modeling machine learning techniques to build separate, high-resolution risk-prediction models for identified population subgroups. This will explicitly map how the TV-mortality relationship differs across these subgroups, revealing subgroup-specific risk 'niches' and the unique combinations of factors that define them, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all population average.

ideas.title: Establish measurement invariance and conceptual equivalence of future-oriented autobiographical thinking across cultures and ages before hypothesis testing
ideas.description: This redesigned study addresses the fatal flaw of assumed construct equivalence by implementing a multi-phase, population-specific foundational research program. Prior to cross-group comparison, it employs in-depth interviews, focus groups, and cognitive pretesting within each target demographic (e.g., specific national/cultural groups, children, older adults) to map the local phenomenology of linking past experiences to future thoughts. This qualitative data then informs the co-creation and rigorous psychometric validation of a culturally and developmentally adapted PASTBI measure within that population. Only after establishing measurement invariance between the original and new instruments can a valid, interpretable test of the PASTBI effect's generalizability be conducted.
